* [x] PCA solves the **standard eigenvalue problem** $\Sigma \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}$; LDA solves the **generalized** one $S_B \mathbf{v} = \lambda S_W \mathbf{v}$.
* [x] The **first principal component** is the eigenvector with the **largest** eigenvalue; PC directions are **mutually orthogonal**.
* [x] **Variance explained** by the $i$-th PC is $\lambda_i / \sum_j \lambda_j$; total of all PCs equals $1$.
* [x] **Cumulative variance threshold** (e.g., 95%) is the standard way to choose $k$ in PCA.
* [x] **LDA dimensions** are bounded by $c - 1$ where $c$ is the number of classes.
* [x] **Fisher's criterion** $J(\mathbf{v}) = \dfrac{\mathbf{v}^T S_B \mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}^T S_W \mathbf{v}}$ is the **objective** that LDA maximizes.
* [x] **$S_W$** measures scatter *within* each class; **$S_B$** measures scatter *between* class means.
* [x] **PCA fails** when classes overlap along the maximum-variance direction — use LDA instead.
* [x] **LDA fails** when $S_W$ is **singular** (e.g., small $n$, high $d$) — apply PCA first as a regularizer.
* [x] **Filter, Wrapper, Embedded** are the three feature-selection families; remember one example of each.
* [x] **Standardization** (Z-score) is recommended when features have different units/scales.
* [x] **Reconstruction error** in PCA equals $1 - \text{CV}_k$ — PCA minimizes this.
* [x] Eigenvectors must be **unit length** ($\Vert \mathbf{v} \Vert = 1$); explicitly state normalization in answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Data Mining: Market Basket Analysis, Customer Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT635 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Data Mining: Market Basket Analysis, Customer Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Market Basket Analysis (MBA)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Market Basket Analysis** is a data mining technique used to uncover associations, co-occurrences, and correlations between items purchased together in transactional databases (point-of-sale data). It is a form of **association rule mining** that identifies patterns of the form **"If item A is bought, then item B is also likely to be bought"**, expressed as the rule $A \Rightarrow B$.

The technique is built upon three foundational metrics:
- **Support** ($s$): Frequency of occurrence of an itemset in the database.
- **Confidence** ($c$): Conditional probability of buying $B$ given that $A$ was bought.
- **Lift** ($l$): Strength of association compared to random chance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In any MBA question, the *support–confidence–lift* trio is mandatory. Skipping any one of the three metrics in a 14-mark derivation will result in a **2–3 mark deduction** under KTU valuation norms.

### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)
Imagine you walk into a **SuperMart in Thiruvananthapuram** and buy *Onam Sadya rice, payasam mix, and banana leaves*. The billing system silently asks: *"What do people who buy these items typically also buy?"* The store observes that 70% of such customers also buy *ghee and jaggery*. The manager then places **ghee and jaggery next to the rice aisle** to cross-sell. This entire chain — from raw transaction to placement strategy — is Market Basket Analysis in action.

MBA is the engine behind:
- **Amazon's "Frequently Bought Together"** recommendations.
- **Flipkart's "Customers who viewed this also viewed"**.
- **Netflix-style cross-promotion** in retail.

---

## 1.2 Customer Segmentation

### Formal Definition
**Customer Segmentation** is the process of dividing a customer base into homogeneous groups (segments) based on shared characteristics such as **demographics, behavior, psychographics, geography, or RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** values. The objective is to enable targeted marketing, personalized recommendations, and resource optimization.

The dominant algorithmic families for segmentation are:
1. **Clustering-based**: K-Means, K-Medoids, Hierarchical Clustering.
2. **Model-based**: Gaussian Mixture Models (EM-based).
3. **Dimensionality-aided**: K-Means on PCA-reduced features.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Highlight:** Segmentation is listed as a *descriptive* data mining application — it does *not* predict a class label; it *discovers* structure in unlabeled data.

### Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)
Think of a **bank** in Kerala. It cannot send the same SMS to a 22-year-old engineering student and a 60-year-old retired government officer. So, the bank divides its 5 lakh customers into groups:
- **Group A:** Young salaried, low balance, high digital usage → push UPI credit cards.
- **Group B:** Senior citizens, high balance, low digital usage → push FD and pension schemes.
- **Group C:** Small business owners → push business loans and POS machines.

This *grouping-by-similarity* is **Customer Segmentation**. The bank's marketing ROI jumps because each message is now *relevant*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why segmentation matters in industry:** Personalized marketing has been shown (industry benchmarks) to deliver **5–8× higher ROI** than mass marketing. This is why MBA + Segmentation are the *first* algorithms taught in any Business Analytics module.

---

## 1.3 Where Do These Techniques Sit in the Data Mining Taxonomy?

| Dimension | Market Basket Analysis | Customer Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| Mining Task | Association Rule Mining | Clustering (Unsupervised) |
| Learning Type | Unsupervised | Unsupervised |
| Output Type | Rules (If–Then) | Group labels + centroids |
| Typical Algorithm | Apriori, FP-Growth | K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical |
| Business Function | Recommendation, Cross-selling | Targeting, Personalization |
| KTU CO Mapping | CO2 – Apply association techniques | CO2 – Apply clustering techniques |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Market Basket Analysis — Operational Pipeline

Market Basket Analysis is executed through a structured pipeline:

### Step 1 — Transaction Database Construction
A transaction $T_i$ is a record of items purchased in a single visit.
A transactional database $D = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_N\}$ contains $N$ transactions.
An **itemset** $X$ is a non-empty subset of all items $I$.

### Step 2 — Frequent Itemset Generation
An itemset $X$ is **frequent** if its support $\geq$ `minsup` (user-defined threshold).

### Step 3 — Strong Rule Generation
A rule $A \Rightarrow B$ is **strong** if both:
- $\text{support}(A \Rightarrow B) \geq \text{minsup}$
- $\text{confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) \geq \text{minconf}$

### Step 4 — Interpretation & Deployment
The strong rules are ranked by **Lift** to identify the *most interesting* associations.

---

## 2.2 The Three Pillars: Support, Confidence, Lift

Let $N$ = total number of transactions in the database $D$.
Let $\text{count}(X)$ = number of transactions containing itemset $X$.

### Mathematical Definitions

For an itemset $X$:

$$\text{Support}(X) = \frac{\text{count}(X)}{N}$$

For an association rule $A \Rightarrow B$ (where $A \cap B = \emptyset$):

$$\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A)}$$

$$\text{Lift}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B)}{\text{Support}(B)} = \frac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A) \cdot \text{Support}(B)}$$

### Interpretation of Lift

| Lift Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $l > 1$ | Positive correlation (A *promotes* B) — *interesting* |
| $l = 1$ | Independence (A and B are unrelated) |
| $l < 1$ | Negative correlation (A *discourages* B) — *substitution* |

---

## 2.3 The Apriori Algorithm — Why It Works

The Apriori algorithm (Agrawal & Srikant, 1994) uses two key properties to prune the search space:

**Downward Closure Property (Apriori Property):**
If an itemset $X$ is frequent, then *all its non-empty subsets* must also be frequent.

**Anti-Monotonicity of Support:**
If an itemset $X$ is infrequent, then *any superset* $Y \supseteq X$ is *also* infrequent.

This property allows Apriori to drastically reduce the candidate generation space from $2^M - 1$ (where $M$ is total distinct items) to a manageable subset.

---

## 2.4 Customer Segmentation — Operational Pipeline

### Step 1 — Feature Engineering
Construct a customer feature vector $\mathbf{x}_i = (x_{i1}, x_{i2}, \ldots, x_{ip})$ using:
- **RFM Metrics:** Recency (days since last purchase), Frequency (total purchases), Monetary (total spend).
- **Demographics:** Age, income, location.
- **Behavioral:** Pages viewed, time-on-app, clickstream.

### Step 2 — Standardization
Apply **Z-score normalization** to each feature so that no feature dominates due to scale:

$$z_{ij} = \frac{x_{ij} - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

### Step 3 — Distance Computation
For two customers $\mathbf{x}_i$ and $\mathbf{x}_j$, K-Means typically uses **Euclidean distance**:

$$d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \sqrt{\sum_{k=1}^{p}(x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$$

### Step 4 — Cluster Assignment & Centroid Update
Each customer is assigned to the nearest centroid, and centroids are recomputed as the mean of all points in the cluster:

$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$$

### Step 5 — Convergence Check
Iterate until cluster assignments stabilize or a maximum iteration count is reached.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Equation / Statement | Boundary / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Support of itemset $X$ | $s(X)$ | $s(X) = \text{count}(X) / N$ | $0 \leq s \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Confidence of rule $A \Rightarrow B$ | $c(A \Rightarrow B)$ | $c = s(A \cup B) / s(A)$ | $0 \leq c \leq 1$ |
| 3 | Lift of rule $A \Rightarrow B$ | $l(A \Rightarrow B)$ | $l = c(A \Rightarrow B) / s(B)$ | $l > 1$ interesting |
| 4 | Conviction of rule | $\text{conv}$ | $\text{conv} = \frac{1 - s(B)}{1 - c(A \Rightarrow B)}$ | $l = 1 \Rightarrow \text{conv} = 1$ |
| 5 | Leverage | $\text{lev}$ | $\text{lev} = s(A \cup B) - s(A)\cdot s(B)$ | Range $[-0.25, 0.25]$ |
| 6 | Apriori Property | — | $X$ frequent $\Rightarrow$ all subsets frequent | Anti-monotone |
| 7 | Z-score Normalization | $z_{ij}$ | $z_{ij} = (x_{ij} - \mu_j)/\sigma_j$ | Unit variance |
| 8 | Euclidean Distance | $d(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | $\sqrt{\sum_k (x_{ik} - x_{jk})^2}$ | $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | Centroid Update | $\boldsymbol{\mu}_k$ | $\frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{i \in C_k} \mathbf{x}_i$ | Within-cluster mean |
| 10 | K-Means Objective (WCSS) | $J$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k \Vert^2$ | Minimize |
| 11 | Elbow Method | $K^*$ | Plot $J$ vs $K$; pick the *elbow* | Heuristic |
| 12 | Silhouette Score | $s_i$ | $(b_i - a_i) / \max(a_i, b_i)$ | Range $[-1, 1]$ |
| 13 | Davies–Bouldin Index | $\text{DB}$ | Lower is better; compact vs separation | $\geq 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Market Basket Analysis drives **cross-selling engines in e-commerce and retail**. Customer Segmentation powers **CRM (Customer Relationship Management), churn prediction pre-processing, and personalized pricing** in telecom, banking, and OTT platforms.

---

## 2.6 Why These Two Techniques Co-Exist in Module 2

Both are **unsupervised learning techniques** that *discover hidden structure* in unlabeled transactional data. MBA discovers *item-level* patterns; Segmentation discovers *customer-level* groups. Together, they form the **descriptive analytics backbone** of any retail/CRM data mining pipeline — exactly the reason KTU places them in Module 2 of PEITT635.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Market Basket Analysis (Full Derivation)

Consider a transactional database $D$ from a small bookstore containing $N = 5$ transactions:

| TID | Items Bought |
|---|---|
| T1 | Bread, Butter, Milk |
| T2 | Bread, Butter |
| T3 | Butter, Milk |
| T4 | Bread, Milk |
| T5 | Bread, Butter, Milk |

We are given:
- `minsup` = 40% (i.e., minimum count = 2 out of 5)
- `minconf` = 60%

### Step A — Compute Support for 1-Itemsets

For each single item, support is the fraction of transactions containing that item:

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread}) = \frac{\text{count(Bread)}}{N} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Butter}) = \frac{\text{count(Butter)}}{N} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Milk}) = \frac{\text{count(Milk)}}{N} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80$$

All three items have support $\geq 0.40$. Hence all are **frequent**.

### Step B — Compute Support for 2-Itemsets

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread}, \text{Butter}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{B, Bu\})}{N} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread}, \text{Milk}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{B, M\})}{N} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

$$\text{Support}(\text{Butter}, \text{Milk}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{Bu, M\})}{N} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60$$

All 2-itemsets satisfy `minsup` = 0.40. Hence all are **frequent**.

### Step C — Compute Support for 3-Itemset

$$\text{Support}(\text{Bread, Butter, Milk}) = \frac{\text{count}(\{B, Bu, M\})}{N} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40$$

The 3-itemset also satisfies `minsup` = 0.40. Hence it is **frequent**.

### Step D — Generate Candidate Association Rules

From the frequent 3-itemset $\{B, Bu, M\}$, we generate candidate rules:

| Rule | Support $s(A \cup B)$ | Confidence Calculation | Lift Calculation |
|---|---|---|---|
| $B \Rightarrow Bu \land M$ | 0.40 | $0.40 / 0.80 = 0.50$ | $0.50 / 0.80 = 0.625$ |
| $Bu \Rightarrow B \land M$ | 0.40 | $0.40 / 0.80 = 0.50$ | $0.50 / 0.80 = 0.625$ |
| $M \Rightarrow B \land Bu$ | 0.40 | $0.40 / 0.80 = 0.50$ | $0.50 / 0.80 = 0.625$ |
| $B, Bu \Rightarrow M$ | 0.40 | $0.40 / 0.60 = 0.667$ | $0.667 / 0.80 = 0.833$ |
| $B, M \Rightarrow Bu$ | 0.40 | $0.40 / 0.60 = 0.667$ | $0.667 / 0.80 = 0.833$ |
| $Bu, M \Rightarrow B$ | 0.40 | $0.40 / 0.60 = 0.667$ | $0.667 / 0.80 = 0.833$ |

### Step E — Detailed Confidence Derivation for Rule $\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow M$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidence}(\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow M) &= \frac{\text{Support}(\{B, Bu\} \cup \{M\})}{\text{Support}(\{B, Bu\})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\text{Support}(\{B, Bu, M\})}{\text{Support}(\{B, Bu\})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2/5}{3/5} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step F — Detailed Lift Derivation for Rule $\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow M$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lift}(\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow M) &= \frac{\text{Confidence}(\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow M)}{\text{Support}(M)} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2/3}{4/5} = \frac{2}{3} \times \frac{5}{4} = \frac{10}{12} \approx 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step G — Identify Strong Rules

Since `minconf` = 60% (= 0.60), the **strong rules** are those with confidence $\geq 0.60$:

| Strong Rule | Confidence | Lift | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\{B, Bu\} \Rightarrow M$ | 0.667 | 0.833 | ✅ Strong |
| $\{B, M\} \Rightarrow Bu$ | 0.667 | 0.833 | ✅ Strong |
| $\{Bu, M\} \Rightarrow B$ | 0.667 | 0.833 | ✅ Strong |
| $B \Rightarrow Bu \land M$ | 0.50 | 0.625 | ❌ Weak |
| $Bu \Rightarrow B \land M$ | 0.50 | 0.625 | ❌ Weak |
| $M \Rightarrow B \land Bu$ | 0.50 | 0.625 | ❌ Weak |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Note that all lifts are **less than 1**, meaning these items appear together *less* than would be expected by chance. Despite high confidence, the **negative lift** signals that the items are *substitutes or randomly co-occurring* rather than truly associated. A perfect KTU answer would flag this and conclude the rules are **statistically weak**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Customer Segmentation using K-Means

Consider 5 customers with two features: **Annual Spend (₹k)** and **Visits per Month**:

| Customer | Annual Spend ($x_1$) | Visits/Month ($x_2$) |
|---|---|---|
| C1 | 10 | 2 |
| C2 | 12 | 3 |
| C3 | 8 | 1 |
| C4 | 50 | 15 |
| C5 | 55 | 14 |

Goal: Form **$K = 2$ clusters**.

### Step 1 — Initialization (Random Centroids)
Choose initial centroids:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)} = (10, 2), \quad \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)} = (50, 15)$$

### Step 2 — First Iteration: Distance Computation

Using Euclidean distance $d = \sqrt{(x_1 - \mu_1)^2 + (x_2 - \mu_2)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}) &= \sqrt{(10-10)^2 + (2-2)^2} = 0 \\[4pt]
d(C1, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)}) &= \sqrt{(10-50)^2 + (2-15)^2} = \sqrt{1600 + 169} = \sqrt{1769} \approx 42.06
\end{aligned}
$$

So $C1$ is assigned to **Cluster 1**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(C2, \boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(0)}) &= \sqrt{(12-10)^2 + (3-2)^2} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24 \\[4pt]
d(C2, \boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(0)}) &= \sqrt{(12-50)^2 + (3-15)^2} = \sqrt{1444 + 144} = \sqrt{1588} \approx 39.85
\end{aligned}
$$

So $C2$ is assigned to **Cluster 1**.

Following similar logic (by inspection of the obvious gap), $C3 \to$ Cluster 1 and $C4, C5 \to$ Cluster 2.

### Step 3 — Recompute Centroids

Cluster 1 contains $\{C1, C2, C3\}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_1^{(1)} = \left(\frac{10+12+8}{3}, \frac{2+3+1}{3}\right) = (10, 2)
$$

Cluster 2 contains $\{C4, C5\}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\mu}_2^{(1)} = \left(\frac{50+55}{2}, \frac{15+14}{2}\right) = (52.5, 14.5)
$$

### Step 4 — Convergence Check
Re-assigning customers produces no change in cluster membership → **Algorithm converges**.

### Step 5 — Interpretation

| Cluster | Members | Profile |
|---|---|---|
| Cluster 1 (Low-Value) | C1, C2, C3 | Occasional, low-spend customers |
| Cluster 2 (High-Value) | C4, C5 | Frequent, high-spend customers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Business Insight:** Marketing budget should target **Cluster 2 with loyalty rewards** (retain high spenders) and **Cluster 1 with discount coupons** (encourage frequency increase). This is the *ROI lever* segmentation unlocks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What

| Criterion | Market Basket Analysis | Customer Segmentation |
|---|---|---|
| Data Type | Transactional (item lists) | Customer feature matrix |
| Typical Size | Very large ($10^6$–$10^9$ rows) | Moderate ($10^3$–$10^6$ rows) |
| Output | Association rules | Cluster labels |
| Scalability Challenge | Exponential item combinations | Distance computation $O(NK)$ per iteration |
| Best Algorithm | FP-Growth (modern) > Apriori | K-Means (large data) > Hierarchical |
| Evaluation | Lift, Confidence, Conviction | Silhouette, Elbow, DB Index |
| Real-Time Use | Recommendation engines | Campaign targeting |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Market Basket Analysis. Explain the role of Support and Confidence in association rule mining.**

**Model Answer:**
Market Basket Analysis (MBA) is a data mining technique that identifies associations between items frequently purchased together in transactional databases. It produces rules of the form $A \Rightarrow B$, where $A$ and $B$ are disjoint itemsets.

- **Support** measures how frequently an itemset appears in the database:
$$\text{Support}(A) = \frac{\text{count}(A)}{N}$$
- **Confidence** measures the reliability of the rule:
$$\text{Confidence}(A \Rightarrow B) = \frac{\text{Support}(A \cup B)}{\text{Support}(A)}$$

A rule is *strong* if both Support $\geq$ `minsup` and Confidence $\geq$ `minconf`. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is Customer Segmentation? List any two clustering algorithms used for segmentation.**

**Model Answer:**
Customer Segmentation is the process of partitioning a customer base into homogeneous groups (segments) based on shared attributes such as demographics, behavior, or RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary) values, so that targeted marketing strategies can be applied to each group.

Two clustering algorithms used:
1. **K-Means Clustering** — partitions customers into $K$ clusters by minimizing within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS).
2. **Hierarchical (Agglomerative) Clustering** — builds a dendrogram by successively merging nearest pairs of customers. (3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔷 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Given the transactional database below, with `minsup` = 30% and `minconf` = 70%, generate all frequent itemsets and find all **strong association rules**. Compute the **Lift** for each strong rule and interpret. **(7 marks)**

| TID | Items |
|---|---|
| T1 | Tea, Sugar, Biscuit |
| T2 | Tea, Biscuit |
| T3 | Sugar, Biscuit |
| T4 | Tea, Sugar |
| T5 | Tea, Sugar, Biscuit |
| T6 | Tea, Biscuit |

**(b)** Explain the **Apriori Algorithm** with its two key properties. State its limitations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Total transactions $N = 6$. Minimum count = $0.30 \times 6 = 1.8$, so an itemset must appear in at least **2 transactions**.

**Step 2 — Frequent 1-itemsets:**

$\text{count}(\text{Tea}) = 5$, $\text{count}(\text{Sugar}) = 4$, $\text{count}(\text{Biscuit}) = 5$. All $\geq 2$. So $L_1 = \{\{T\}, \{S\}, \{B\}\}$.

**Step 3 — Candidate 2-itemsets and Support:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Support}(\{T,S\}) &= 3/6 = 0.50 \geq 0.30 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Frequent} \\
\text{Support}(\{T,B\}) &= 5/6 \approx 0.833 \geq 0.30 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Frequent} \\
\text{Support}(\{S,B\}) &= 3/6 = 0.50 \geq 0.30 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Frequent}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $L_2 = \{\{T,S\}, \{T,B\}, \{S,B\}\}$.

**Step 4 — Candidate 3-itemset:**

$\text{Support}(\{T,S,B\}) = 2/6 \approx 0.333 \geq 0.30 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Frequent}$. So $L_3 = \{\{T,S,B\}\}$.

**Step 5 — Strong Rules from $L_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidence}(T \Rightarrow S) &= \frac{2/6}{5/6} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak} \\
\text{Confidence}(S \Rightarrow T) &= \frac{2/6}{4/6} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.50 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak} \\
\text{Confidence}(B \Rightarrow T) &= \frac{2/6}{5/6} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak} \\
\text{Confidence}(T \Rightarrow S \land B) &= \frac{2/6}{5/6} = 0.40 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak} \\
\text{Confidence}(S \Rightarrow T \land B) &= \frac{2/6}{4/6} = 0.50 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak} \\
\text{Confidence}(B \Rightarrow T \land S) &= \frac{2/6}{5/6} = 0.40 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strong Rules from $L_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Confidence}(T \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{5/6}{5/6} = 1.00 \geq 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{STRONG} \\
\text{Confidence}(B \Rightarrow T) &= \frac{5/6}{5/6} = 1.00 \geq 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{STRONG} \\
\text{Confidence}(S \Rightarrow T) &= \frac{3/6}{4/6} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{STRONG} \\
\text{Confidence}(T \Rightarrow S) &= \frac{3/6}{5/6} = 0.60 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak} \\
\text{Confidence}(S \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{3/6}{4/6} = 0.75 \geq 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{STRONG} \\
\text{Confidence}(B \Rightarrow S) &= \frac{3/6}{5/6} = 0.60 < 0.70 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Weak}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Lift Computation for Strong Rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lift}(T \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{1.00}{5/6} = \frac{6}{5} = 1.20 \\
\text{Lift}(B \Rightarrow T) &= \frac{1.00}{5/6} = 1.20 \\
\text{Lift}(S \Rightarrow T) &= \frac{0.75}{5/6} = \frac{0.75 \times 6}{5} = 0.90 \\
\text{Lift}(S \Rightarrow B) &= \frac{0.75}{5/6} = 0.90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation:**

- $T \Rightarrow B$ and $B \Rightarrow T$ have **Lift = 1.20 > 1** → **Positive association** (Tea and Biscuit are bought together more than by chance). This is the **most useful business rule** — bundle them or place them adjacently.
- $S \Rightarrow T$ and $S \Rightarrow B$ have **Lift = 0.90 < 1** → **Negative association** (Sugar and Tea/Biscuit appear together *less* than by chance). Rule is statistically weak despite high confidence.

**(7 marks awarded for systematic computation and interpretation.)**

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Apriori Algorithm** is a classical algorithm for mining frequent itemsets in a transactional database. It operates in an iterative, level-wise manner.

**Pseudo-Steps:**
1. Set $k = 1$.
2. Generate candidate 1-itemsets $C_1$.
3. Scan database to compute support. Keep only itemsets with support $\geq$ `minsup` to form $L_1$.
4. Repeat:
   - Generate $C_{k+1}$ by **join** of $L_k$ with itself.
   - Apply **Apriori pruning** to remove candidates whose subsets are not in $L_k$.
   - Scan database, compute support, form $L_{k+1}$.
5. Stop when $L_k = \emptyset$.

**Key Property 1 — Downward Closure (Apriori Property):**
If an itemset is frequent, all its non-empty subsets are also frequent.
*Equivalently:* If a subset of an itemset is infrequent, the itemset itself cannot be frequent (anti-monotone).

**Key Property 2 — Candidate Pruning:**
Any $(k+1)$-itemset containing an infrequent $k$-subset is pruned, drastically reducing the search space from $2^M$ candidates.

**Limitations of Apriori:**
1. **Multiple database scans** — One scan per level $k$ is expensive for large $k$.
2. **Huge candidate set generation** — Even after pruning, $C_k$ can be very large.
3. **High memory consumption** for large candidate pools.
4. **Difficulty with low `minsup`** — Candidate explosion when threshold is low.

*(7 marks complete with 3 marks for steps + 2 marks for properties + 2 marks for limitations.)*

---

### 🔷 Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain **Customer Segmentation** in detail. Discuss the **RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** model with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **K-Means clustering algorithm** for segmentation. How is the optimal number of clusters ($K$) chosen? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Customer Segmentation** is the analytical process of dividing an organization's customers into distinct groups that share similar characteristics (demographic, geographic, psychographic, or behavioral). The objective is to deliver **personalized marketing, improve customer retention, and optimize marketing spend**.

**RFM Model** is a quantitative segmentation framework that classifies customers based on three transactional metrics:

| Metric | Definition | Business Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Recency (R)** | Days since the customer's last purchase | Lower = more engaged |
| **Frequency (F)** | Total number of purchases in a defined period | Higher = more loyal |
| **Monetary (M)** | Total money spent by the customer | Higher = more valuable |

**Example — A Kerala-based Online Grocery App:**
Consider 5 customers with the following RFM values (Recency in days, Frequency in 6 months, Monetary in ₹k):

| Customer | R | F | M |
|---|---|---|---|
| C1 | 5 | 20 | 25 |
| C2 | 60 | 5 | 8 |
| C3 | 10 | 15 | 22 |
| C4 | 90 | 3 | 4 |
| C5 | 7 | 22 | 28 |

After **standardization** and **K-Means clustering** (with $K = 3$):

- **Champions:** C1, C5 (low R, high F, high M) → Reward with loyalty program.
- **Loyal Customers:** C3 (moderate R, moderate F, moderate M) → Upsell premium products.
- **At-Risk / Hibernating:** C2, C4 (high R, low F, low M) → Win-back campaigns and discount offers.

This RFM-driven segmentation enables the marketing team to **target each group with tailored messages**, improving conversion rates by 3–5× compared to mass marketing.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for definition + 2 marks for RFM explanation + 2 marks for example interpretation.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**K-Means Clustering Algorithm** is an **unsupervised, iterative, partition-based** clustering technique used for customer segmentation.

**Steps of K-Means:**

1. **Choose $K$** — the desired number of clusters.
2. **Initialize centroids** — either randomly or via K-Means++.
3. **Assignment Step:** For each data point $\mathbf{x}_i$, compute Euclidean distance to all $K$ centroids and assign it to the nearest cluster:
$$C_k^{(t)} = \{\mathbf{x}_i : \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t)}\Vert^2 \leq \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_j^{(t)}\Vert^2 \;\forall j \neq k\}$$
4. **Update Step:** Recompute each centroid as the mean of points in its cluster:
$$\boldsymbol{\mu}_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)}\vert}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k^{(t)}} \mathbf{x}_i$$
5. **Repeat Steps 3–4** until centroids stop moving (convergence) or max iterations reached.

**Objective Function (WCSS):**
$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\sum_{\mathbf{x}_i \in C_k}\Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \boldsymbol{\mu}_k\Vert^2$$
K-Means minimizes $J$.

**Choosing Optimal $K$:**

**(i) Elbow Method:** Plot $J$ vs $K$. The point where the curve forms an "elbow" (rate of decrease sharply slows) is the optimal $K$.

**(ii) Silhouette Score:**
$$s_i = \frac{b_i - a_i}{\max(a_i, b_i)}$$
where $a_i$ = average distance to points in same cluster, $b_i$ = minimum average distance to points in other clusters. The $K$ with the **highest average silhouette score** is optimal.

**(iii) Gap Statistic:** Compares total intra-cluster variation to its expected value under null reference distribution.

*(7 marks — 2 marks for steps + 2 marks for objective + 3 marks for choosing K.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute Lift** after listing strong rules → loses 2 marks. Lift is *mandatory* for full credit.
> 2. **Mixing up support thresholds** — Students often compute `minsup` count as `minsup × |D|` incorrectly. Always show: `min_count = ceil(minsup × N)`.
> 3. **Skipping standardization** in K-Means → features with large scales dominate distance, giving wrong clusters. Always mention Z-score normalization.
> 4. **Stating "K = number of categories"** without justifying via Elbow/Silhouette → examiner deducts 1–2 marks. Always pair the choice with a *method*.
> 5. **Confusing support of a rule vs. support of an itemset** → support of rule $A \Rightarrow B$ = support of $A \cup B$.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Market Basket Analysis** mines association rules of the form $A \Rightarrow B$ from transactional data.
- **Support** = frequency of itemset / total transactions; **Confidence** = conditional probability; **Lift** = strength vs. random chance.
- A rule is **strong** only if Support $\geq$ `minsup` AND Confidence $\geq$ `minconf`.
- **Lift > 1** → positive correlation; **Lift = 1** → independence; **Lift < 1** → negative correlation (substitution).
- The **Apriori Algorithm** uses the **Downward Closure Property** (anti-monotonicity of support) to prune the search space.
- Apriori's limitations (multiple DB scans, large candidate sets) motivate **FP-Growth**.
- **Customer Segmentation** is an unsupervised clustering task that groups customers by similarity for targeted marketing.
- **RFM (Recency, Frequency, Monetary)** is the industry-standard feature framework for retail segmentation.
- **K-Means** is the workhorse clustering algorithm — requires **standardization** of features.
- Optimal $K$ is chosen via **Elbow Method**, **Silhouette Score**, or **Gap Statistic**.
- **Evaluation metrics for clustering**: Silhouette, Davies–Bouldin Index, WCSS.
- MBA and Segmentation together form the **descriptive analytics backbone** of any retail/CRM pipeline.
- Common pitfall: confusing **rule support** with **itemset support** — they are the same entity but often mis-stated.
- Always interpret **Lift** directionally in business context — it's the metric that makes a rule *useful*, not just *frequent*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Predictive Analytics: Concepts and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT635 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Predictive Analytics: Concepts and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Predictive Analytics** is the branch of advanced analytics that uses historical data, statistical algorithms, machine learning techniques, and mathematical models to forecast the likelihood of future outcomes, behaviors, or trends. It answers the question *"What is most likely to happen?"* by exploiting patterns discovered in past observations.

In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned framework, Predictive Analytics is positioned as the **prognostic layer** of the analytics maturity pyramid — sitting above Descriptive Analytics (What happened?) and Diagnostic Analytics (Why did it happen?), and below Prescriptive Analytics (What should we do?).

$$\text{Analytics Maturity} = \{ \text{Descriptive} \prec \text{Diagnostic} \prec \text{Predictive} \prec \text{Prescriptive} \}$$

> [!NOTE] **Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Standard)**
> - **Target Variable (Y):** The future outcome we want to predict (e.g., will the customer churn?).
> - **Predictor / Feature Variable (X):** The historical input variables used to forecast Y.
> - **Model (f):** The mathematical function $\hat{Y} = f(X)$ that maps inputs to predicted outputs.
> - **Training Set:** The historical dataset used to *teach* the model.
> - **Test Set:** The unseen dataset used to *evaluate* the model.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!TIP] **Analogy: The Experienced Farmer**
> Imagine an old farmer who has been cultivating the same land for **40 years**. He doesn't need a weather app — by looking at the sky, feeling the wind, and remembering past monsoons, he says: *"It will rain in 3 days."* He is essentially performing **Predictive Analytics**:
> - His **memory of past seasons** = Historical Data
> - His **pattern recognition** = Statistical / ML Algorithm
> - His **forecast of rain** = Predicted Future Outcome
> - His **act of watering the field preemptively** = Business Decision Based on Prediction
>
> Predictive Analytics automates exactly this "experienced farmer's instinct" using mathematics instead of gut feeling, and at a scale no human can match.

---

### 1.3 Why Predictive Analytics Matters in 2024

| Industry | Predictive Question | Business Value |
|---|---|---|
| Banking | Will this loan default? | **Loss reduction** |
| Healthcare | Will this patient be readmitted? | **Cost savings** |
| Retail | What will the customer buy next? | **Revenue uplift** |
| Manufacturing | When will this machine fail? | **Downtime reduction** |
| Telecom | Will the user churn this quarter? | **Retention savings** |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Predictive Analytics is the backbone of Industry 4.0, smart-city dashboards, and AI-driven decision support systems. KTU examiners explicitly look for the ability to map a real-world business problem to its correct predictive technique.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Predictive Analytics Problems

Predictive Analytics broadly splits into **four families** of problems. Every KTU exam question falls into one of these buckets.

#### A. Classification (Predicting a Category)
The target variable $Y$ is **discrete** — e.g., Yes/No, Spam/Not Spam, Disease A / B / C.

- **Binary Classification:** Two classes (e.g., fraud vs. genuine).
- **Multi-Class Classification:** More than two classes (e.g., digit recognition 0–9).

#### B. Regression (Predicting a Continuous Value)
The target variable $Y$ is **continuous** — e.g., temperature, sales revenue, house price.

#### C. Time Series Forecasting (Predicting Sequential Values)
Predictions over a temporal index — e.g., stock price next week, electricity load tomorrow.

#### D. Survival / Churn Analysis (Predicting Time-to-Event)
Predicting *when* an event will occur — e.g., customer lifetime, equipment failure time.

---

### 2.2 The Predictive Analytics Lifecycle (CRISP-DM Style)

KTU 2024 strongly emphasizes this **six-step lifecycle** for full marks.

1. **Problem Definition** — Translate a business question into a predictive task.
2. **Data Collection & Understanding** — Gather and explore historical data (EDA).
3. **Data Preparation** — Clean, normalize, handle missing values, feature engineer.
4. **Model Building** — Choose algorithm (e.g., Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Random Forest, XGBoost).
5. **Model Evaluation** — Quantify accuracy using metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, RMSE).
6. **Deployment & Monitoring** — Push to production and track drift over time.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **All formulas below are KTU high-yield. Memorize the entire table.**

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Metric / Concept} & \textbf{Formula} & \textbf{Use Case} \\
\hline
\text{Accuracy} & \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} & \text{Balanced classes} \\
\hline
\text{Precision} & \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP} & \text{Spam filter (cost of FP high)} \\
\hline
\text{Recall (Sensitivity)} & \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN} & \text{Cancer detection (cost of FN high)} \\
\hline
\text{F1-Score} & 2 \cdot \dfrac{P \cdot R}{P + R} & \text{Imbalanced classes} \\
\hline
\text{MAE} & \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i\vert & \text{Regression, robust to outliers} \\
\hline
\text{MSE} & \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 & \text{Regression, penalizes large errors} \\
\hline
\text{RMSE} & \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2} & \text{Regression, same unit as } Y \\
\hline
\text{R-Squared} & 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2} & \text{Goodness of fit (0 to 1)} \\
\hline
\text{Simple Linear Model} & \hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X & \text{Single predictor regression} \\
\hline
\text{Log-Odds (Logistic)} & \log\!\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X & \text{Classification baseline} \\
\hline
\text{Bias-Variance} & \text{Error} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2 & \text{Model tuning intuition} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!NOTE] **LaTeX Tip:** The absolute value notation in `MAE` uses $\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i\vert$ (the *\vert* command), **never** the keyboard pipe character `|`, because in a KTU-style markdown table the pipe is the column separator and will break the table parser.

---

### 2.4 The "Why" and "How" — Why Each Metric Exists

- **Why Accuracy fails on imbalanced data:** If 99% of transactions are genuine, a model that predicts *always genuine* scores 99% accuracy but is useless. This is why **Precision & Recall** exist.
- **Why RMSE is preferred over MSE:** RMSE brings the error back to the **original unit of Y**, making it interpretable by business stakeholders.
- **Why R-Squared is bounded in [0, 1]:** It represents the **proportion of variance explained** by the model. Closer to 1 = better fit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Confusion Matrix — The Foundation of Classification Metrics

For a **binary classifier**, every prediction falls into one of four cells:

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}
\hline
 & \textbf{Actual Positive} & \textbf{Actual Negative} \\
\hline
\textbf{Predicted Positive} & TP & FP \\
\hline
\textbf{Predicted Negative} & FN & TN \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

Where:
- **TP** = True Positive (correctly predicted positive)
- **TN** = True Negative (correctly predicted negative)
- **FP** = False Positive (Type I error — false alarm)
- **FN** = False Negative (Type II error — missed detection)

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Confusion Matrix to All Metrics

> **KTU-Style Problem:** A loan-default classifier was tested on 200 customers. Results:
> - True Positive (correctly flagged defaulters) = **30**
> - False Positive (genuine flagged as defaulters) = **20**
> - False Negative (defaulters missed) = **10**
> - True Negative (genuine correctly cleared) = **140**

#### Step 1 — Verify Totals

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Total} &= TP + TN + FP + FN \\
&= 30 + 140 + 20 + 10 \\
&= 200 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Accuracy

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{30 + 140}{200} \\
&= \frac{170}{200} \\
&= 0.85 = 85\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The model is correct on 85% of all applicants.

#### Step 3 — Compute Precision

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{30}{30 + 20} \\
&= \frac{30}{50} \\
&= 0.60 = 60\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** Out of all customers *the model flagged as defaulters*, only 60% actually defaulted.

#### Step 4 — Compute Recall (Sensitivity)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Recall} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{30}{30 + 10} \\
&= \frac{30}{40} \\
&= 0.75 = 75\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The model successfully caught 75% of *all true defaulters*.

#### Step 5 — Compute F1-Score

$$\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{P + R} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.60 \cdot 0.75}{0.60 + 0.75} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.45}{1.35} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.3333 \\
&= 0.6667 = 66.67\%
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 6 — Analytical Summary Table

| Metric | Value | Business Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | 85% | Looks great |
| Precision | 60% | Many false alarms — wastes investigation cost |
| Recall | 75% | Misses 25% of defaulters — risky |
| F1-Score | 66.67% | Single balanced number — moderate model |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Insight:** A naive reading of "85% accuracy" would make us declare the model production-ready. But the deeper diagnostic reveals **40% of defaulters flagged are false alarms** (low precision) and **25% of true defaulters are missed** (low recall). This is exactly why KTU examiners test all four metrics, not just accuracy.

---

### 3.3 Worked Regression Example — MAE, MSE, RMSE

> **Problem:** Actual house prices vs. predicted prices for 5 houses:
> $y = [200, 250, 300, 350, 400]$ (in lakhs ₹)
> $\hat{y} = [210, 240, 310, 330, 420]$

#### Step 1 — Compute Residuals

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = [-10, \; +10, \; -10, \; +20, \; -20]$$

#### Step 2 — MAE (Mean Absolute Error)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MAE} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert e_i \mid \\
&= \frac{\vert -10 \vert + \vert 10 \vert + \vert -10 \vert + \vert 20 \vert + \vert -20 \vert}{5} \\
&= \frac{10 + 10 + 10 + 20 + 20}{5} \\
&= \frac{70}{5} \\
&= 14 \;\text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 3 — MSE (Mean Squared Error)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{MSE} &= \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 \\
&= \frac{(-10)^2 + (10)^2 + (-10)^2 + (20)^2 + (-20)^2}{5} \\
&= \frac{100 + 100 + 100 + 400 + 400}{5} \\
&= \frac{1100}{5} \\
&= 220 \;\text{lakhs}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> Note: Squaring the unit makes MSE non-interpretable in lakhs. **This is precisely why RMSE exists.**

#### Step 4 — RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error)

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{RMSE} &= \sqrt{\text{MSE}} \\
&= \sqrt{220} \\
&\approx 14.83 \;\text{lakhs}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** On average, the model is off by approximately **₹14.83 lakhs** per house.

#### Step 5 — Comparative Insight Table

| Metric | Value | Penalty Style |
|---|---|---|
| MAE | 14.00 | Linear — treats all errors equally |
| MSE | 220.00 | Quadratic — penalizes big errors heavily |
| RMSE | 14.83 | Same unit as Y — best for stakeholder reporting |

> [!NOTE] **Why MAE (14) < RMSE (14.83)?** Because RMSE squares the larger errors (the 20-lakh misses) before averaging, giving them a disproportionately higher weight. RMSE is therefore the *worst-case-aware* metric.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Predictive Analytics Workflow (CRISP-DM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Business Problem Definition"] --> B["Data Collection and EDA"]
    B --> C["Data Preprocessing and Feature Engineering"]
    C --> D["Train Test Split"]
    D --> E["Model Selection Classification Regression TimeSeries"]
    E --> F["Model Training using Historical Data"]
    F --> G["Model Evaluation Accuracy Precision Recall RMSE"]
    G --> H{Performance Acceptable}
    H -- Yes --> I["Deploy Model to Production"]
    H -- No --> J["Tune Hyperparameters or Try New Algorithm"]
    J --> E
    I --> K["Monitor Predictions and Detect Drift"]
    K --> L["Retrain Periodically with New Data"]
    L --> E

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Types of Analytics Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Descriptive Analytics What Happened"] --> P2["Diagnostic Analytics Why Did It Happen"]
    P2 --> P3["Predictive Analytics What Will Happen"]
    P3 --> P4["Prescriptive Analytics What Should We Do"]
    P3 --> A1["Classification Categorical Y"]
    P3 --> A2["Regression Continuous Y"]
    P3 --> A3["Time Series Forecasting Temporal Y"]
    P3 --> A4["Survival Analysis Time to Event Y"]

    style P3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,stroke-width:3px
    style P4 fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Predictive System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Raw Data Sources DB Logs IoT CRM"] --> ETL["Data Pipeline ETL Layer"]
    ETL --> FS["Feature Store Cleaned Variables"]
    FS --> MOD["Model Training Engine"]
    MOD --> EVAL["Evaluation Module Metrics Validation"]
    EVAL --> API["Prediction API Service"]
    API --> UI["Dashboard for End Users"]
    API --> DEC["Automated Business Decisions"]
    DEC --> FB["Feedback Loop Capture Actuals"]
    FB --> MOD

    style MOD fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    style API fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px
    style FB fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT] **Reading the Diagrams for KTU Exams:**
> - **Diagram 1** demonstrates the **iterative nature** of model building — the loop back to "Model Selection" after poor evaluation.
> - **Diagram 2** answers the *most common KTU 2-mark question*: "Distinguish between Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive Analytics."
> - **Diagram 3** shows the **production-grade architecture** — the Feedback Loop (FB) is what separates a *student project* from a *real-world deployment*, and is often a 5-mark differentiator in KTU answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define Predictive Analytics. Differentiate it from Descriptive Analytics with one example.**

**Model Answer:**
Predictive Analytics uses historical data and statistical/ML algorithms to forecast future outcomes. Descriptive Analytics only summarizes past events.
*Example:* Descriptive → *"Sales were ₹10 lakh last month."* Predictive → *"Sales will be ₹12 lakh next month."*

*(Marks: Definition 2 + Example 1 = 3 marks)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`
**List any three real-world applications of Predictive Analytics in the BFSI (Banking, Financial Services, Insurance) sector.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Credit Risk Scoring** — Predicting loan default probability.
2. **Fraud Detection** — Flagging anomalous transactions in real time.
3. **Customer Churn Prediction** — Forecasting which account will close.

*(1 mark per correct application = 3 marks)*

---

### 📕 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Students routinely lose **2–3 marks** by listing algorithms without stating *when to use them*. Always mention **one use-case per algorithm**.
> - Another common pitfall is writing only the **formula** without the **interpretation** ("What does this number mean to the business?"). KTU 2024 explicitly rewards interpretation.
> - For confusion-matrix problems, **always verify that TP + TN + FP + FN = Total**. Examiners check this and deduct 1 mark if totals don't match.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **six-step lifecycle of a Predictive Analytics project** with a suitable real-world example. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The lifecycle of a Predictive Analytics project follows the CRISP-DM framework:

1. **Business Problem Definition (1 mark):** Identify the predictive task. *Example:* A retail chain wants to predict which customers will respond to a marketing campaign.
2. **Data Collection (1 mark):** Gather historical customer data — demographics, past purchases, response history.
3. **Data Preparation (1 mark):** Handle missing values, normalize numerical fields, encode categorical variables, perform feature engineering.
4. **Model Building (1.5 marks):** Choose an algorithm — e.g., **Logistic Regression** for binary response (Yes/No). Train on 80% of data.
5. **Model Evaluation (1.5 marks):** Use **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score** on the 20% test set.
6. **Deployment & Monitoring (1 mark):** Deploy via REST API, monitor monthly for concept drift, retrain quarterly.

---

**(b)** A spam detection system was tested on 500 emails with the following results:
- TP = 120, FP = 30, FN = 40, TN = 310.
  Compute **Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score**, and comment on which metric is most critical for a spam filter. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify Totals:**

$$TP + TN + FP + FN = 120 + 310 + 30 + 40 = 500 \;\checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{120 + 310}{500} = \frac{430}{500} = 0.86 = 86\%$$

**Step 3 — Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.80 = 80\%$$

**Step 4 — Recall:**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{120}{120 + 40} = \frac{120}{160} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 5 — F1-Score:**

$$F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.80 \cdot 0.75}{0.80 + 0.75} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.60}{1.55} = 2 \cdot 0.3871 = 0.7742 = 77.42\%$$

**Comment (1.5 marks):** For a spam filter, **Precision is the most critical metric**. A *false positive* (a legitimate email marked as spam) is highly damaging — the user may miss an important email and lose trust in the system forever. Recall matters less here because missing a spam email (FN) is a minor inconvenience compared to losing an important legitimate email.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Compare **Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, and Prescriptive Analytics** with one industry example each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Type | Question Answered | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| Descriptive | What happened? | Monthly sales dashboard showing ₹10 lakh revenue in Q1 |
| Diagnostic | Why did it happen? | Root-cause analysis showing sales dipped due to a failed discount campaign |
| Predictive | What will happen? | Forecast that Q2 sales will be ₹12 lakh based on trend |
| Prescriptive | What should we do? | Recommendation to launch a new combo offer to push Q2 sales to ₹14 lakh |

The four layers form an **analytics maturity ladder** — organizations begin with descriptive reporting and progressively climb toward prescriptive decision automation. *(7 marks = 1.75 per row × 4)*

---

**(b)** For the following actual vs predicted regression values, compute **MAE, MSE, RMSE**, and state which is most appropriate for stakeholder reporting. *(7 marks)*

Actual $y = [100, 200, 300, 400, 500]$
Predicted $\hat{y} = [110, 190, 290, 380, 480]$

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Residuals:**

$$e = y - \hat{y} = [-10, +10, +10, +20, +20]$$

**Step 2 — MAE:**

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{10 + 10 + 10 + 20 + 20}{5} = \frac{70}{5} = 14.0$$

**Step 3 — MSE:**

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{100 + 100 + 100 + 400 + 400}{5} = \frac{1100}{5} = 220.0$$

**Step 4 — RMSE:**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{220.0} \approx 14.83$$

**Step 5 — Recommendation (1.5 marks):** **RMSE** is most appropriate for stakeholder reporting because its unit matches the original target variable (here, ₹ lakhs), making it directly interpretable. MAE is robust to outliers but under-penalizes large errors, while MSE inflates large errors quadratically and is hard to interpret.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Exam-Eve Glance)**
>
> - ✅ Predictive Analytics = **forecasting future Y** from historical X using statistical/ML models.
> - ✅ Sits **third** in the analytics maturity ladder: Descriptive → Diagnostic → **Predictive** → Prescriptive.
> - ✅ Two main problem types: **Classification (categorical Y)** and **Regression (continuous Y)**.
> - ✅ **Accuracy alone is misleading** for imbalanced data — always report Precision, Recall, F1.
> - ✅ For classification, **Precision matters when FP is costly** (spam filter); **Recall matters when FN is costly** (cancer detection).
> - ✅ For regression, prefer **RMSE** for reporting (same unit as Y); use **MAE** when outliers are present.
> - ✅ R-Squared ∈ [0, 1] = proportion of variance explained by the model.
> - ✅ Always **verify confusion-matrix totals** match the dataset size before computing metrics.
> - ✅ The **CRISP-DM 6-step lifecycle** (Problem → Data → Prep → Model → Evaluate → Deploy) is the gold-standard answer structure for 7-mark theory questions.
> - ✅ A **Feedback Loop** in the production architecture is what distinguishes academic projects from real-world predictive systems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Learning: Linear Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forests
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT635 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning: Linear Regression, Decision Trees, Random Forests

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Supervised Learning — The Master Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Supervised Learning is a Machine Learning paradigm in which a model is trained on a labelled dataset, meaning each training example is paired with an output label. The model learns a mapping function $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$ such that given a new input $\mathbf{x}$, it can predict the corresponding output $\hat{y}$ with minimum generalization error.*

The two principal sub-tasks under Supervised Learning are:

| Sub-Task | Output Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | Discrete (Categorical) | Spam detection, Disease diagnosis |
| **Regression** | Continuous (Numerical) | Price prediction, Temperature forecasting |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, **Module 3** explicitly targets three algorithms that bridge these sub-tasks: *Linear Regression* (Regression), *Decision Trees* (Both), and *Random Forests* (Both).

### Intuition: The Student–Teacher Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a child learning to identify fruits. The mother holds up an *apple* and says *"Apple"*, holds a *banana* and says *"Banana"*. After thousands of such labelled examples, the child learns the mapping between visual features (shape, color, texture) and fruit names. When shown a new fruit, the child can *predict* its label. This is **Supervised Learning** — the labels act as the "supervisor."

---

## 1.2 Linear Regression

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Linear Regression is a parametric supervised regression algorithm that models the relationship between a scalar dependent variable $y$ and one or more independent variables $\mathbf{x}$ by fitting a linear equation to the observed data.*

### Mathematical Form

For a single feature:

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x + \varepsilon$$

For $n$ features (Multivariate / Multiple Linear Regression):

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \beta_2 x_2 + \dots + \beta_n x_n + \varepsilon$$

where:
- $y$ = Dependent (target) variable
- $x_i$ = Independent (predictor) variables
- $\beta_0$ = **Intercept** (value of $y$ when all $x_i = 0$)
- $\beta_i$ = **Slope coefficients** (change in $y$ per unit change in $x_i$)
- $\varepsilon$ = **Error term** (irreducible noise)

### Intuition: The Best-Fit Line Analogy

> [!TIP]
> Imagine plotting students' **study hours** (x-axis) against **exam scores** (y-axis) on a graph. The points form a cloud. Linear regression draws the *single straight line* through this cloud that comes closest to every point — minimizing the total vertical distance. This line then predicts scores for new students.

---

## 1.3 Decision Trees

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *A Decision Tree is a non-parametric supervised learning algorithm that recursively partitions the feature space into homogeneous sub-regions using a tree-structured model of decisions, where internal nodes represent feature tests, branches represent outcomes, and leaf nodes represent class labels or regression values.*

### Intuition: The 20-Questions Game

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** A doctor diagnosing a patient asks a sequence of *yes/no questions*: *"Do you have a fever? → Yes → Is the fever above 101°F? → Yes → Is there a rash? → No → Diagnosis: Flu."* Each question splits the remaining possibilities, narrowing down until a confident diagnosis is reached. A Decision Tree learns this *optimal sequence* of questions from historical patient data.

### Splitting Criteria

| Criterion | Formula | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | ID3, C4.5 |
| **Information Gain** | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in A} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | ID3 |
| **Gini Index** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$ | CART |

---

## 1.4 Random Forests

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *A Random Forest is an ensemble learning method that constructs a multitude of Decision Trees during training and outputs the mode of the classes (classification) or mean prediction (regression) of the individual trees, thereby reducing variance and preventing overfitting.*

### Intuition: The Wisdom of the Crowd

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world analogy:** Instead of asking **one** doctor for a diagnosis, consult **100 doctors** independently and take the majority verdict. While any single doctor may err, the collective decision is far more accurate. A Random Forest is precisely this — it trains hundreds of Decision Trees on **random subsets** of data and features, then aggregates their predictions.

### Key Properties

- **Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating):** Each tree trains on a random sample drawn *with replacement*.
- **Feature Randomness:** At each split, only a random subset of features is considered.
- **Out-of-Bag (OOB) Samples:** ~37% of data is left out per tree, serving as a free validation set.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Regression — Mechanism & Cost Function

### The Least Squares Objective

The goal is to find coefficients $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ that minimize the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_i - \boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{x}_i \right)^2$$

The corresponding **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** — the standard cost function:

$$MSE = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why square the errors?** (1) It penalizes large errors more heavily than small ones. (2) It makes the function differentiable and convex. (3) It avoids cancellation of positive and negative residuals.

### Assumptions of Linear Regression (Gauss–Markov Conditions)

1. **Linearity:** The relationship between $X$ and $y$ is linear.
2. **Independence:** Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Homoscedasticity:** Constant variance of residuals across all levels of $X$.
4. **Normality:** Residuals follow a Normal distribution $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.
5. **No Multicollinearity:** Independent variables are not highly correlated.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Linear Regression)

| Quantity | Formula | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Hypothesis | $\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x$ | Scalar |
| Cost Function (MSE) | $J(\beta) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | Unit of $y^2$ |
| Gradient (Slope) | $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_j} = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | — |
| Coefficient (Slope) | $\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | — |
| Intercept | $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$ | Unit of $y$ |
| Normal Equation | $\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$ | Vector |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | $[0, 1]$ |
| Adjusted $R^2$ | $\bar{R}^2 = 1 - (1 - R^2)\frac{m-1}{m-n-1}$ | $[0, 1]$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Normal Equation requires matrix inversion of $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$, which is **computationally expensive** for large $n$ (roughly $O(n^3)$). For $n > 10{,}000$, **Gradient Descent** is preferred.

---

## 2.3 Decision Trees — Splitting Mechanisms

### 2.3.1 Entropy — Measure of Impurity

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$$

where $p_i$ is the proportion of class $i$ in set $S$, and $c$ is the number of classes.

- $H(S) = 0$ → Pure node (all samples same class)
- $H(S) = 1$ → Maximum impurity (binary 50–50 split)

### 2.3.2 Information Gain

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Gain = Parent Entropy − Weighted Child Entropy**. The attribute $A$ with the **highest IG** is selected at each node (Greedy, top-down induction — the ID3 algorithm by Quinlan, 1986).

### 2.3.3 Gini Index

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^2$$

- $Gini = 0$ → Pure node
- $Gini = 0.5$ → Maximum impurity for binary classification

> [!TIP]
> **Gini vs Entropy:** Both yield similar trees. Gini is computationally cheaper (no logarithm), so CART and scikit-learn's default `DecisionTreeClassifier` prefer it.

### 2.3.4 Regression Trees — Variance Reduction

For regression trees, splits maximize the **Reduction in Variance (RV)**:

$$RV = Var(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Var(S_v)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Decision Trees)

| Criterion | Formula | Range | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3, C4.5 |
| Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $[0, 1]$ | ID3 |
| Gain Ratio | $GainRatio = \frac{IG(S,A)}{SplitInfo(S,A)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | C4.5 |
| Gini Index | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $[0, 1 - \frac{1}{c}]$ | CART |
| Variance Reduction | $\Delta Var = Var(S) - \sum w_v \cdot Var(S_v)$ | $\geq 0$ | Regression Trees |

---

## 2.5 Random Forests — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.5.1 Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating)

For a dataset $D$ of size $m$, generate $B$ bootstrap samples $D_b$ by sampling $m$ instances **with replacement** from $D$. Each $D_b$ trains an independent tree $T_b$.

### 2.5.2 Aggregation Rule

- **Classification (Majority Voting):**

$$\hat{y} = \text{mode}\left\{ T_1(\mathbf{x}), T_2(\mathbf{x}), \dots, T_B(\mathbf{x}) \right\}$$

- **Regression (Averaging):**

$$\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b(\mathbf{x})$$

### 2.5.3 Out-of-Bag (OOB) Error

Since each bootstrap sample leaves out ~36.8% of the original data, these untouched samples can act as a free validation set. The OOB error provides an unbiased estimate of the generalization error.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Random Forests are widely used in **remote sensing** (land-cover classification), **biomedical signal processing** (ECG anomaly detection), **financial credit scoring**, and **bioinformatics** (gene selection). The method is robust to noise, handles missing values, and provides built-in feature importance scores via **Mean Decrease in Impurity (MDI)**.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Random Forests)

| Quantity | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Trees | $B$ | Hyperparameter (e.g., 100, 500) |
| Bootstrap Sample Size | $m$ (equal to original) | With replacement |
| OOB Fraction | $1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{m}\right)^m \approx 0.368$ | Unused per tree |
| Max Features per Split | $\sqrt{n}$ (classification) or $\frac{n}{3}$ (regression) | Common heuristic |
| Final Prediction (Class.) | $\hat{y} = \text{mode}(T_b(\mathbf{x}))$ | Hard voting |
| Final Prediction (Reg.) | $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{B}\sum T_b(\mathbf{x})$ | Averaging |
| Variance Reduction | $Var(\bar{T}) \approx \frac{\rho \sigma^2}{B} + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}$ | $\rho$ = tree correlation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Random Forests do **NOT** eliminate bias — they primarily reduce **variance**. If the base trees are systematically wrong, the forest will be too.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Simple Linear Regression Coefficients (Least Squares)

### Problem Setup

Given $m$ data points $\{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \dots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\}$, we seek $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ such that $\hat{y}^{(i)} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x^{(i)}$ minimizes:

$$J(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)}\right)^2$$

### Step 1 — Substitute the Hypothesis

$$J(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)}\right]^2$$

### Step 2 — Compute Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\beta_0$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} 2\left[y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)}\right](-1)$$

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_0} = 0$:

$$-\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)}\right] = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} y^{(i)} = m\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}$$

Dividing by $m$:

$$\bar{y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivative w.r.t. $\beta_1$

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = \frac{1}{2m}\sum_{i=1}^{m} 2\left[y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)}\right](-x^{(i)})$$

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial \beta_1} = 0$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}\left[y^{(i)} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 x^{(i)}\right] = 0$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}\left[y^{(i)} - \bar{y} + \beta_1 \bar{x} - \beta_1 x^{(i)}\right] = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}(y^{(i)} - \bar{y}) - \beta_1 \sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x}) = 0$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $\beta_1$

$$\beta_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m} x^{(i)}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})(y^{(i)} - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}(x^{(i)} - \bar{x})^2}$$

### Step 6 — Recover $\beta_0$

$$\beta_0 = \bar{y} - \beta_1 \bar{x}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** closed-form solutions. They are *unique* and *unbiased* under the Gauss–Markov assumptions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Normal Equation for Multivariate Regression

### Matrix Formulation

Stack the data into matrices: $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times (n+1)}$, $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times 1}$, $\boldsymbol{\beta} \in \mathbb{R}^{(n+1) \times 1}$.

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & x_1^{(1)} & x_2^{(1)} & \cdots & x_n^{(1)} \\ 1 & x_1^{(2)} & x_2^{(2)} & \cdots & x_n^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & x_1^{(m)} & x_2^{(m)} & \cdots & x_n^{(m)} \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} y^{(1)} \\ y^{(2)} \\ \vdots \\ y^{(m)} \end{bmatrix}$$

The cost function in matrix form:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m}(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y})^\top(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{y})$$

### Step 1 — Expand the Quadratic Form

$$J(\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \frac{1}{2m}\left[\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\beta} - 2\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^\top \mathbf{y}\right]$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Gradient

Using the matrix calculus identities $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}) = \mathbf{A}^\top$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\beta}}(\boldsymbol{\beta}^\top \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\beta}) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\beta}$ (symmetric $\mathbf{A}$):

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\beta}} J = \frac{1}{2m}\left[2\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - 2\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}\right] = \frac{1}{m}\left[\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Set Gradient to Zero

$$\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} - \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y} = 0$$

$$\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\beta}$

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{y}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Condition:** The matrix $\mathbf{X}^\top \mathbf{X}$ must be **invertible** (non-singular). This requires the features to be linearly independent and $m \geq n+1$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Entropy and Information Gain Calculation

### Worked Example

Consider a dataset $S$ with 14 instances: 9 of class **Yes**, 5 of class **No**.

### Step 1 — Compute Parent Entropy

$$H(S) = -\left(\frac{9}{14}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \left(\frac{5}{14}\right)\log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right)$$

$$H(S) = -0.643 \times \log_2(0.643) - 0.357 \times \log_2(0.357)$$

$$H(S) = -0.643 \times (-0.6375) - 0.357 \times (-1.4854)$$

$$H(S) = 0.4099 + 0.5303 = 0.9402 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Child Entropies for Attribute $A$ (say, "Outlook")

Assume $A$ partitions $S$ into:
- Sunny: 5 samples (2 Yes, 3 No)
- Overcast: 4 samples (4 Yes, 0 No)
- Rain: 5 samples (3 Yes, 2 No)

$$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\frac{2}{5} - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\frac{3}{5} = -0.4(-1.322) - 0.6(-0.737) = 0.5288 + 0.4422 = 0.9710$$

$$H(S_{Overcast}) = -\frac{4}{4}\log_2\!\frac{4}{4} - \frac{0}{4}\log_2\!\frac{0}{4} = 0 + 0 = 0$$

$$H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\frac{3}{5} - \frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\frac{2}{5} = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.9710$$

### Step 3 — Compute Weighted Child Entropy

$$H(S \mid A) = \frac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \frac{4}{14}(0) + \frac{5}{14}(0.9710)$$

$$H(S \mid A) = 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6936 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Information Gain

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A) = 0.9402 - 0.6936 = 0.2466 \text{ bits}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $0.2466 > 0$, attribute $A$ provides information gain. In ID3, we choose the attribute with the **maximum IG** as the root node.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Linear Regression | Decision Tree | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Parametric | Non-Parametric | Non-Parametric Ensemble |
| **Task** | Regression (primarily) | Both | Both |
| **Interpretability** | High (closed-form) | Very High (rules) | Low (black-box) |
| **Bias** | Moderate | Low | Low |
| **Variance** | Low | High | Low–Moderate |
| **Overfitting Risk** | Low | High (unpruned) | Low |
| **Handles Non-linearity** | No | Yes | Yes |
| **Training Speed** | Fast (Normal Eq.) | Moderate | Slow (many trees) |
| **Prediction Speed** | Very Fast | Very Fast | Slow (averaging) |
| **Feature Scaling Required** | Yes | No | No |
| **Handles Missing Values** | No | Limited | Yes (OOB) |
| **Output** | Continuous value | Class / Value | Class / Value (aggregated) |
