**Step 2 — Total model fits (2 marks):**
$$N_{\text{fits}} = K \cdot \vert \mathcal{G} \vert = 5 \times 9 = 45 \text{ fits}$$

**Step 3 — Total wall-clock time (2 marks):**
$$T_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{fits}} \times t = 45 \times 2.4 = 108 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The total wall-clock time is **108 seconds ≈ 1.8 minutes**, which is highly feasible. However, doubling the number of hyperparameters to 4 (each with 3 candidates) increases $\vert \mathcal{G} \vert$ to $3^4 = 81$, total fits to $405$, and wall-clock time to $16.2$ minutes — illustrating the curse of dimensionality.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CV with Train-Test Split (–2 marks):** Many students write "we use CV to test the model." Wrong — CV is for *validation during model selection*. The test set is touched only once at the very end.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Bessel's Correction (–1 mark):** Using $\frac{1}{K}$ instead of $\frac{1}{K-1}$ in variance calculation. KTU examiners specifically check this in numerical questions.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Computational Cost (–1 mark):** A Grid Search answer that does not mention total fits $K \cdot \prod n_j$ or curse of dimensionality is incomplete.
>
> 4. **Stratified vs Plain K-Fold Mix-up (–2 marks):** Writing Stratified K-Fold for regression (which has no classes) — examiners deduct heavily.
>
> 5. **No Diagram in Part B (a) (–2 marks):** A 7-mark procedure question without a flowchart/diagram is automatically capped at 5 marks.
>
> 6. **Data Leakage Ignorance (–1 mark):** Not mentioning that scaling/imputation must be fit *inside each fold*, not before splitting. This is the #1 favourite KTU examiner trap for "applied" questions.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **K-Fold CV** splits data into $K$ folds; trains $K$ times; averages validation scores: $\bar{s}_{CV} = \frac{1}{K} \sum s_k$.
- [x] **Variance** uses $\frac{1}{K-1}$ (Bessel's correction), not $\frac{1}{K}$.
- [x] **Stratified K-Fold** is mandatory for classification, especially with class imbalance.
- [x] **Time-Series Split** uses forward-chaining folds — never random shuffle for time data.
- [x] **LOOCV** is $K=N$ — unbiased but high variance and expensive.
- [x] **Practical default:** $K = 5$ or $K = 10$ balances bias, variance, and compute.
- [x] **Grid Search** exhaustively evaluates all hyperparameter combos in a Cartesian product grid.
- [x] **Total fits** = $K \cdot \prod_{j=1}^{m} n_j$ — grows exponentially with $m$.
- [x] **Grid Search + CV** together provide unbiased hyperparameter selection — never tune on the test set.
- [x] **Nested CV** = outer (unbiased estimate) + inner (model selection via Grid Search).
- [x] **Grid Search limitations:** curse of dimensionality, no continuous refinement, ignores interactions.
- [x] **No data leakage:** scale/impute *inside* each fold on training data only.
- [x] **95% CI** = $\bar{s}_{CV} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sigma_{CV}/\sqrt{K}$ — use this to compare competing models statistically.
- [x] **Always include a diagram** in 7-mark Part B answers — a clean flowchart earns at least 2 marks.
- [x] **Mention computational cost** whenever discussing Grid Search — examiners explicitly test for this.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies in Predictive Analytics: Financial Forecasting, Health Analytics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT635 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies in Predictive Analytics: Financial Forecasting, Health Analytics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Predictive Analytics** is the branch of advanced analytics that uses historical data, statistical algorithms, machine learning techniques, and mathematical models to forecast the likelihood of future outcomes, behaviours, or trends. A **Case Study** in predictive analytics is a documented, end-to-end engineering narrative that captures the business problem, dataset characteristics, exploratory analysis, feature engineering, model selection, evaluation, deployment, and operational impact of a real-world forecasting system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 of PEITT635 - *Data Analytics* requires students to interpret, compare, and critically evaluate predictive analytics pipelines used in **Financial Forecasting** (time-series stock prediction, credit risk scoring, fraud detection) and **Health Analytics** (disease prediction, hospital readmission, epidemic modelling).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, predictive case studies are assessed under **Course Outcome CO3**: *"Apply predictive modelling techniques to solve real-world analytics problems and interpret results with appropriate evaluation metrics."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a doctor who, after seeing 10,000 patients over 20 years, can predict with surprising accuracy that an elderly patient with high blood pressure, a specific ECG pattern, and a family history of cardiac disease is **80% likely** to suffer a cardiac event within the next year. The doctor is essentially running a *learned predictive model in her head*.

Similarly, a **financial analyst** who has studied 30 years of market data can forecast that a stock with a particular *moving-average crossover*, *P/E ratio*, and *trading volume signature* is likely to rise. Predictive analytics simply **formalises** this human intuition into reproducible, mathematically-rigorous pipelines.

### 1.3 The Two Domains at a Glance

| Attribute | Financial Forecasting | Health Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Type** | Time-series, transactional, market microstructure | EHR records, imaging, genomics, vitals |
| **Target Variable** | Price, default probability, fraud flag | Disease presence, readmission, mortality |
| **Core Challenge** | Non-stationarity, noise, regime shifts | Class imbalance, privacy, interpretability |
| **Key Metrics** | RMSE, MAPE, Sharpe Ratio, AUC | Sensitivity, Specificity, F1, AUC |
| **Regulation** | Basel III, SEBI, RBI norms | HIPAA, DISHA, FDA |
| **Model Bias Risk** | Market shocks | Demographic disparity |

## 1.4 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently frame **14-mark Part B questions** around case studies because they test the student's ability to **integrate concepts** across data preprocessing, modelling, and evaluation — a skill that isolated definition questions cannot measure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Predictive Analytics Pipeline

Every predictive case study — whether in finance or health — follows the same five-stage architecture:

1. **Problem Definition** — Translate a business question into a measurable ML objective (regression vs. classification).
2. **Data Acquisition & EDA** — Collect, clean, visualise; handle missing values, outliers, and class imbalance.
3. **Feature Engineering** — Construct domain-specific signals (e.g., **technical indicators** in finance; **comorbidity indices** in healthcare).
4. **Model Training & Selection** — Choose between statistical, classical ML, or deep-learning approaches.
5. **Evaluation & Deployment** — Apply domain-appropriate metrics; monitor for *model drift*.

## 2.2 Financial Forecasting: Theoretical Backbone

### 2.2.1 Time-Series Components

A financial time-series $y_t$ can be decomposed as:

$$y_t = T_t + S_t + C_t + \epsilon_t$$

Where $T_t$ is the **trend**, $S_t$ is the **seasonal** component, $C_t$ is the **cyclical** component, and $\epsilon_t$ is **white noise** $\sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$.

### 2.2.2 The ARIMA(p,d,q) Model

**AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average** is the workhorse of financial forecasting:

$$\Delta^d y_t = c + \sum_{i=1}^{p}\phi_i \Delta^d y_{t-i} + \sum_{j=1}^{q}\theta_j \epsilon_{t-j} + \epsilon_t$$

Where:
- $p$ = autoregressive order
- $d$ = differencing order (to achieve stationarity)
- $q$ = moving-average order
- $\phi_i$ = AR coefficients
- $\theta_j$ = MA coefficients

### 2.2.3 Credit Risk Scoring (Logistic Regression)

The probability of **loan default** is modelled as:

$$P(\text{Default}=1 \mid \mathbf{x}) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_1 + \dots + \beta_n x_n)}}$$

The log-odds (logit) transformation gives:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{1-P}\right) = \beta_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{n}\beta_k x_k$$

## 2.3 Health Analytics: Theoretical Backbone

### 2.3.1 Binary Disease Prediction

For a binary outcome (Disease / No Disease), the **confusion matrix** underpins all metrics:

| | Predicted Positive | Predicted Negative |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Positive** | True Positive (TP) | False Negative (FN) |
| **Actual Negative** | False Positive (FP) | True Negative (TN) |

### 2.3.2 Survival Analysis (Cox Proportional Hazards)

For **time-to-event** data (e.g., patient survival, hospital readmission):

$$h(t \mid \mathbf{x}) = h_0(t) \cdot \exp\!\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n}\beta_k x_k\right)$$

Where $h(t \mid \mathbf{x})$ is the **hazard function** at time $t$ given covariates $\mathbf{x}$, and $h_0(t)$ is the **baseline hazard**.

### 2.3.3 ROC-AUC Intuition

The **Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve** measures the model's ability to discriminate between classes across all possible classification thresholds. An AUC of **0.5** indicates random guessing; **1.0** indicates perfect separation.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The following table consolidates every formula you must memorise for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on predictive case studies.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Domain | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Finance | $[0, \infty)$ |
| 2 | Root MSE | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\text{MSE}}$ | Finance | Same scale as $y$ |
| 3 | Mean Absolute Error | $\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\vert y_i - \hat{y}_i\vert$ | Finance | Same scale as $y$ |
| 4 | Mean Absolute Percentage Error | $\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\vert \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right\vert$ | Finance | $[0, 100]\%$ |
| 5 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Health | $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | Recall (Sensitivity) | $\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Health | $[0, 1]$ |
| 7 | Specificity | $\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | Health | $[0, 1]$ |
| 8 | F1-Score | $F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}$ | Health | $[0, 1]$ |
| 9 | Logistic Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Both | $(0, 1)$ |
| 10 | ARIMA forecast | $\hat{y}_{t+h}$ from ARIMA(p,d,q) | Finance | Index form |
| 11 | Sharpe Ratio | $S = \frac{\mathbb{E}[R_p - R_f]}{\sigma_p}$ | Finance | Dimensionless |
| 12 | Cox Hazard Ratio | $\text{HR} = e^{\beta_k}$ | Health | $[0, \infty)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These metrics are deployed in production at scale — RMSE drives algorithmic trading backtests; F1-Score underpins clinical decision-support systems globally.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Why RMSE Penalises Large Errors More

The Mean Squared Error is preferred over MAE in financial forecasting because large price deviations (crashes) must be penalised aggressively. We derive the relationship between RMSE, variance, and bias.

### Step 1 — Start with the squared-error definition

$$\text{MSE} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

### Step 2 — Decompose the error

Let $y_i = f(x_i) + \epsilon_i$ where $\epsilon_i$ is irreducible noise with $\mathbb{E}[\epsilon_i] = 0$. The prediction $\hat{y}_i$ is an estimator of $f(x_i)$. Then:

$$y_i - \hat{y}_i = \bigl(f(x_i) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}_i]\bigr) + \bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat{y}_i] - \hat{y}_i\bigr) + \epsilon_i$$

### Step 3 — Square both sides and take expectation

\begin{aligned}
\mathbb{E}\!\left[(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2\right] &= \mathbb{E}\!\left[\bigl(f(x_i) - \mathbb{E}[\hat{y}_i]\bigr)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}\!\left[\bigl(\mathbb{E}[\hat{y}_i] - \hat{y}_i\bigr)^2\right] + \mathbb{E}[\epsilon_i^2] \\
&= \text{Bias}^2(\hat{y}_i) + \text{Var}(\hat{y}_i) + \sigma^2
\end{aligned}

### Step 4 — Conclude the Bias-Variance-Noise Decomposition

$$\boxed{\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Irreducible Error}}$$

This decomposition proves that **RMSE cannot be reduced below the noise floor** $\sigma$, regardless of model complexity — a critical insight in financial forecasting where irreducible market noise dominates.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: MAPE and Its Symmetry Problem

The MAPE metric is widely used in business reporting. However, it has a hidden *asymmetry* flaw.

### Step 1 — Definition

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\vert \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right\vert$$

### Step 2 — Substitute a 10% over-prediction vs under-prediction

**Case A**: $y_i = 100$, $\hat{y}_i = 110$ (over-predict by 10)

$$\text{APE}_A = \left\vert \frac{100 - 110}{100}\right\vert \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Case B**: $y_i = 110$, $\hat{y}_i = 100$ (under-predict by 10)

$$\text{APE}_B = \left\vert \frac{110 - 100}{110}\right\vert \times 100 = 9.09\%$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

The same magnitude of error produces **different percentages** depending on whether the prediction overshoots or undershoots. This is why the **symmetric MAPE (sMAPE)** is preferred in KTU-validated exam answers:

$$\text{sMAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{2 \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i\vert}{\vert y_i\vert + \vert \hat{y}_i\vert}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Confusion Metrics for a Health Case Study

Suppose a hospital deploys a model to predict **diabetes onset** in 1,000 patients. The confusion matrix obtained is:

| | Predicted Diabetic | Predicted Healthy |
|---|---|---|
| **Actually Diabetic** | 180 | 20 |
| **Actually Healthy** | 30 | 770 |

### Step 1 — Extract TP, FP, FN, TN

$$TP = 180,\quad FP = 30,\quad FN = 20,\quad TN = 770$$

### Step 2 — Verify totals

$$TP + FN = 200 \text{ (actual positives)}, \quad FP + TN = 800 \text{ (actual negatives)}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Precision

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{180}{180 + 30} = \frac{180}{210} = 0.857$$

### Step 4 — Compute Recall (Sensitivity)

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{180}{180 + 20} = \frac{180}{200} = 0.900$$

### Step 5 — Compute Specificity

$$\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{770}{770 + 30} = \frac{770}{800} = 0.9625$$

### Step 6 — Compute F1-Score

\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 0.857 \times 0.900}{0.857 + 0.900} \\
&= \frac{1.5426}{1.757} \\
&\approx 0.878
\end{aligned}

### Step 7 — Compute Accuracy

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + FP + FN + TN} = \frac{180 + 770}{1000} = 0.950$$

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical Interpretation:** With sensitivity of **0.90**, the model correctly flags 9 out of 10 truly diabetic patients. With specificity of **0.96**, it rarely produces false alarms. This balance is critical for *clinical decision-support*.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: ARIMA(1,1,1) Forecast

Given a financial time-series where $\phi_1 = 0.7$, $\theta_1 = 0.2$, current value $y_t = 150$, prior value $y_{t-1} = 148$, prior residual $\epsilon_{t-1} = 1.5$, and current residual $\epsilon_t = 0$, predict $y_{t+1}$.

### Step 1 — Apply the ARIMA(1,1,1) equation after differencing

After first differencing, $\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}$. The model is:

$$\Delta y_t = \phi_1 \Delta y_{t-1} + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + \epsilon_t$$

### Step 2 — Compute $\Delta y_{t-1}$

$$\Delta y_{t-1} = y_{t-1} - y_{t-2}$$

Assume $y_{t-2} = 145$, so $\Delta y_{t-1} = 148 - 145 = 3$.

### Step 3 — Forecast $\Delta y_{t+1}$ (assuming future residuals are zero)

\begin{aligned}
\Delta \hat{y}_{t+1} &= \phi_1 \Delta y_t + \theta_1 \epsilon_t \\
&= 0.7 \times (150 - 148) + 0.2 \times 0 \\
&= 0.7 \times 2 \\
&= 1.4
\end{aligned}

### Step 4 — Recover the level forecast

$$\hat{y}_{t+1} = y_t + \Delta \hat{y}_{t+1} = 150 + 1.4 = 151.4$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Predictive Analytics Case Study Pipeline

The following diagram shows the unified pipeline used in both financial forecasting and health analytics case studies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Business Problem] --> A1[Define ML Objective]
    A1 --> A2[Data Collection]
    A2 --> A3[Exploratory Data Analysis]
    A3 --> A4{Data Quality OK?}
    A4 -- No --> A5[Clean and Impute]
    A5 --> A3
    A4 -- Yes --> A6[Feature Engineering]
    A6 --> A7[Train Validation Test Split]
    A7 --> A8[Model Selection]
    A8 --> A9{Regression or Classification?}
    A9 -- Regression --> A10[Linear, ARIMA, XGBoost]
    A9 -- Classification --> A11[Logistic, Random Forest, Neural Net]
    A10 --> A12[Compute RMSE MAE MAPE]
    A11 --> A13[Compute Precision Recall F1 AUC]
    A12 --> A14[Cross Validation and Tuning]
    A13 --> A14
    A14 --> A15{Model Meets SLA?}
    A15 -- No --> A8
    A15 -- Yes --> A16[Deployment and Monitoring]
    A16 --> A17[Drift Detection]
    A17 --> A18[Periodic Retraining]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Financial Forecasting Case Study Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Market Data Feed] --> B2[Price Volume News]
    B2 --> B3[Technical Indicators Engine]
    B3 --> B4[Feature Store]
    B4 --> B5[ARIMA Model]
    B4 --> B6[LSTM Deep Model]
    B4 --> B7[Ensemble Layer]
    B5 --> B7
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[Price Forecast Signal]
    B8 --> B9[Risk Management Module]
    B9 --> B10[Trade Execution]
    B10 --> B11[Performance Dashboard]
    B11 --> B12[Sharpe Ratio and Drawdown]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Health Analytics Case Study Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[EHR System] --> C2[Demographics Labs Vitals]
    C1 --> C3[Medical Imaging PACS]
    C2 --> C4[Patient Feature Matrix]
    C3 --> C5[Image Preprocessing]
    C5 --> C6[CNN Feature Extractor]
    C4 --> C7[Tabular Model]
    C6 --> C8[Multimodal Fusion Layer]
    C7 --> C8
    C8 --> C9[Disease Risk Score]
    C9 --> C10{Threshold Check}
    C10 -- High Risk --> C11[Clinician Alert]
    C10 -- Low Risk --> C12[Standard Care Path]
    C11 --> C13[Feedback Loop to Model]
    C12 --> C13
    C13 --> C14[Model Retraining Pipeline]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Comparison of Two Domains

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIN[Financial Forecasting Domain]
        F1[Goal Predict Price or Default] --> F2[Data OHLCV News Macro]
        F2 --> F3[Models ARIMA GARCH LSTM XGBoost]
        F3 --> F4[Metrics RMSE MAPE Sharpe AUC]
        F4 --> F5[Output Trading Signal or Risk Score]
    end
    subgraph HEALTH[Health Analytics Domain]
        H1[Goal Predict Disease or Readmission] --> H2[Data EHR Imaging Genomics]
        H2 --> H3[Models Logistic RF CNN Survival]
        H3 --> H4[Metrics Sensitivity Specificity F1 AUC]
        H4 --> H5[Output Clinical Decision Support]
    end
    FIN -. Shared Methodology .-> HEALTH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The shared methodology arrow indicates that although the *inputs* and *outputs* differ, the underlying **statistical learning principles** — train/test split, cross-validation, evaluation metrics — remain identical. This is why KTU examiners emphasise both domains in Module 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE). Why is it preferred over RMSE in business forecasting despite its asymmetry problem?**

**Model Answer:**

MAPE is defined as:

$$\text{MAPE} = \frac{100}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left\vert \frac{y_i - \hat{y}_i}{y_i}\right\vert$$

It expresses error as a **percentage**, making it dimensionless and easily interpretable by non-technical stakeholders. For example, a MAPE of 5% means predictions deviate from actuals by 5% on average. This **scale-independence** is why business analysts prefer MAPE when comparing forecasts across stocks with vastly different price levels (e.g., ₹50 stock vs. ₹5,000 stock). However, its asymmetry — over-prediction produces higher MAPE than under-prediction of equal magnitude — is a known limitation.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between Precision and Recall in the context of a predictive health analytics system for cancer detection.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ |
| **Question Answered** | *Of all patients flagged positive, how many truly have cancer?* | *Of all patients who truly have cancer, how many did we catch?* |
| **Clinical Priority** | Avoids unnecessary biopsies | Avoids missed diagnoses |
| **Trade-off** | Higher precision → fewer false alarms | Higher recall → fewer missed cancers |

In cancer screening, **Recall is prioritised** over Precision because a missed cancer (FN) is far more harmful than a false alarm (FP) that triggers additional tests.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Internal Choice Option 1

**A leading Indian bank wants to build a credit-scoring model to predict loan default. Answer the following:**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the complete predictive analytics pipeline for this case study, including data sources, feature engineering, model selection, and evaluation metrics.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Definition:**
Binary classification: Predict $y \in \{0, 1\}$ where $y=1$ indicates loan default within 12 months.

**Step 2 — Data Sources:**

- Applicant demographics (age, income, employment)
- Credit bureau history (CIBIL score, past defaults)
- Loan attributes (amount, tenure, interest rate)
- Macroeconomic indicators (repo rate, unemployment)

**Step 3 — Feature Engineering:**

- **Debt-to-Income (DTI) ratio**: $\text{DTI} = \frac{\text{Total Monthly Debt}}{\text{Monthly Income}}$
- **Loan-to-Value (LTV) ratio**: $\text{LTV} = \frac{\text{Loan Amount}}{\text{Collateral Value}}$
- One-hot encoding for categorical variables (occupation, region)

**Step 4 — Model Selection:**

Three candidate models are evaluated:

1. **Logistic Regression** — interpretable baseline
2. **Random Forest** — captures non-linear interactions
3. **XGBoost** — high performance on tabular data

**Step 5 — Evaluation Metrics:**

Because the dataset is imbalanced (defaults ~ 8%), **Accuracy alone is misleading**. Use:

- **AUC-ROC** for discrimination ability
- **Precision-Recall AUC** for imbalanced data
- **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) statistic** for risk scoring

$$\text{KS} = \max_{t} \vert \text{CDR}(t) - \text{BDR}(t)\vert$$

where CDR is the cumulative default rate and BDR is the cumulative bad rate.

**Step 6 — Regulatory Compliance:**

Per **RBI guidelines**, the model must be validated on out-of-time data, and feature importance must be explainable for audit.

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A logistic regression model trained on 5,000 loan applicants yields the following confusion matrix on a test set of 1,000 applicants:

| | Predicted Default | Predicted No Default |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Default** | 120 | 30 |
| **Actual No Default** | 50 | 800 |

**Calculate Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Accuracy. Interpret which metric is most critical from a regulatory perspective.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Extract values:**

$$TP = 120,\quad FN = 30,\quad FP = 50,\quad TN = 800$$

**Step 2 — Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{120}{120 + 50} = \frac{120}{170} = 0.706$$

**Step 3 — Recall:**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{120}{120 + 30} = \frac{120}{150} = 0.800$$

**Step 4 — F1-Score:**

\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \times 0.706 \times 0.800}{0.706 + 0.800} \\
&= \frac{1.1296}{1.506} \\
&\approx 0.750
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{120 + 800}{1000} = 0.920$$

**Step 6 — Regulatory Interpretation:**

From a **regulatory and fairness perspective**, **Recall (0.80)** is most critical because it represents the bank's ability to identify truly risky borrowers. A low recall means the bank approves loans that will default, causing financial loss. However, **Precision (0.706)** is also important — low precision means the bank rejects creditworthy applicants, raising *fair-lending* concerns. The F1-Score of **0.75** reflects a balanced trade-off.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Internal Choice Option 2

**A hospital wants to predict 30-day patient readmission using Electronic Health Records (EHR). Answer the following:**

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain how you would handle class imbalance, missing values, and feature selection for this health analytics case study. Justify each choice.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Class Imbalance:**

Typically, only 15-20% of patients are readmitted. Without correction, the model predicts "no readmission" for everyone and achieves 80%+ accuracy while being useless.

**Techniques:**

- **SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique)** — generates synthetic minority samples
- **Class weighting** — penalise misclassification of minority class with weight $w_j = \frac{n}{K \cdot n_j}$ where $K$ is the number of classes
- **Threshold tuning** — lower the decision threshold from 0.5 to 0.3 to favour recall

**Step 2 — Missing Values:**

EHR data has 20-40% missingness due to optional tests. Strategies:

- **MCAR (Missing Completely at Random)**: Listwise deletion or mean imputation
- **MAR (Missing at Random)**: Multiple imputation by chained equations (MICE)
- **MNAR (Missing Not at Random)**: Domain-aware imputation (e.g., missing lab value → assume normal unless patient is in ICU)

**Step 3 — Feature Selection:**

- **Clinical features**: Charlson Comorbidity Index, Elixhauser comorbidity score
- **Statistical**: L1 (Lasso) regularisation drives irrelevant feature weights to zero
- **Model-based**: Random Forest feature importance, XGBoost gain

**Step 4 — Justification Summary:**

Each choice balances **clinical interpretability** (clinicians must trust the model) with **predictive performance** (high recall to catch at-risk patients).

---

#### (b) [7 Marks] A deployed readmission model produces the following ROC curve statistics: AUC = 0.82, Sensitivity = 0.75, Specificity = 0.78 at the chosen threshold. Compute the Youden's J statistic and explain its clinical utility. If the hospital wants to catch at least 90% of readmissions, what trade-off must be accepted?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Youden's J Statistic:**

$$J = \text{Sensitivity} + \text{Specificity} - 1 = 0.75 + 0.78 - 1 = 0.53$$

**Step 2 — Clinical Utility of Youden's J:**

Youden's J quantifies the **maximum vertical distance** between the ROC curve and the diagonal (random classifier) line. A J of **0.53** indicates the model has moderate discriminative ability at the optimal threshold. It is widely used in medicine to select the **single best operating point** for a binary test.

**Step 3 — Trade-off for 90% Sensitivity:**

To catch 90% of readmissions (Sensitivity = 0.90), the decision threshold must be **lowered**. This will inevitably increase False Positives and **decrease Specificity**. The trade-off accepted is:

- **More false alarms** → care teams investigate patients who would not have been readmitted
- **Higher workload** for discharge planners
- **Possible alert fatigue** among clinical staff

**Quantitatively**, if Specificity drops from 0.78 to approximately 0.55 (a typical decline), then:

$$\text{New FPR} = 1 - \text{Specificity} = 0.45$$

This means 45% of non-readmitted patients receive unnecessary post-discharge interventions.

**Step 4 — Mitigations:**

- **Two-stage triage**: Use ML as first filter, then clinician review
- **Cost-sensitive learning**: Assign asymmetric misclassification costs

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Recall** — Examiners frequently deduct 1-2 marks for interchanging these. Memorise: *Precision = positive predictive value; Recall = sensitivity.*
> 2. **Ignoring Class Imbalance** — Reporting 95% accuracy on a 95:5 imbalanced dataset is a guaranteed mark-loser. Always mention imbalance handling.
> 3. **Using RMSE on Categorical Data** — RMSE is only valid for continuous targets. For classification, use log-loss or cross-entropy.
> 4. **Forgetting Domain Context** — In financial questions, always mention **stationarity** (ADF test). In health questions, always mention **clinical interpretability** and **HIPAA/DISHA compliance**.
> 5. **Skipping Units and Ranges** — AUC is bounded in $[0, 1]$, not $[0, 100]$. RMSE has the same units as $y$. Examiners expect these annotations.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ Predictive analytics uses **historical data + statistical/ML models** to forecast future outcomes.
- ✅ The **canonical pipeline** has 5 stages: Problem → Data → Features → Model → Evaluate → Deploy.
- ✅ **Financial forecasting** is primarily a **regression / time-series problem** — key metrics: RMSE, MAPE, Sharpe Ratio.
- ✅ **Health analytics** is primarily a **classification / survival problem** — key metrics: Sensitivity, Specificity, F1, AUC.
- ✅ **ARIMA(p,d,q)** is the foundational time-series model: $p$ = AR order, $d$ = differencing, $q$ = MA order.
- ✅ **Logistic regression** outputs a probability via the **sigmoid function**: $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$.
- ✅ **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation of all classification metrics: TP, FP, FN, TN.
- ✅ **Precision-Recall trade-off** is central: tune threshold based on domain cost of errors.
- ✅ **AUC-ROC** = Area Under ROC; **0.5 = random**, **1.0 = perfect**.
- ✅ **MAPE is asymmetric** — over-prediction penalised more than under-prediction; prefer **sMAPE** for fairness.
- ✅ **Bias-Variance-Noise decomposition**: $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \sigma^2$.
- ✅ **Cox Proportional Hazards** is the standard survival model for time-to-event health data.
- ✅ **Class imbalance** is the #1 issue in health analytics — use SMOTE, class weights, or threshold tuning.
- ✅ **Stationarity** (constant mean/variance over time) is required for ARIMA — apply ADF test.
- ✅ Always interpret metrics in **domain context** — examiners reward contextualised answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Big Data Analytics: Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL Databases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT635 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Big Data Analytics: Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL Databases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Big Data Analytics — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Aligned):**
> Big Data Analytics refers to the systematic process of examining, transforming, and modeling large, complex, and rapidly growing datasets (terabytes to petabytes) to uncover hidden patterns, unknown correlations, market trends, customer preferences, and other useful business intelligence — using specialized distributed storage, parallel processing, and NoSQL storage technologies.

### The 5 V's of Big Data (KTU High-Yield)

| Dimension | Definition | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume** | Quantity of data generated | **TB / PB / ZB** |
| **Velocity** | Speed at which data is produced | **Real-time streaming** |
| **Variety** | Different data formats | Structured, Semi, Unstructured |
| **Veracity** | Trustworthiness of data | Quality and provenance |
| **Value** | Business insight extracted | Actionable intelligence |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4 specifically tests **Hadoop Ecosystem**, **Apache Spark Architecture**, and **NoSQL Database Paradigms**. Focus your revision around these three pillars.

---

## 1.2 Apache Hadoop — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> Hadoop is an open-source, Java-based, distributed storage and processing framework that allows for the distributed processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers using simple programming models (primarily **MapReduce**). It is designed to scale up from a single server to thousands of machines, each offering local computation and storage.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Library System

Imagine you have **10,000 books** in a library and **100 students** who want to read them all in one day. Instead of giving one student all the books (which is impossible), you:

1. **Split** the books into 100 smaller piles (Sharding).
2. **Distribute** each pile to one student (Distribution).
3. Each student reads their pile in parallel (Parallelism).
4. They write summaries on small cards (Map phase).
5. All summaries are combined into a final report (Reduce phase).

**Hadoop = The Librarian**. It manages splitting, distributing, parallel processing, fault tolerance, and final aggregation.

---

## 1.3 Apache Spark — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> Apache Spark is an open-source, distributed, in-memory data processing engine that provides high-level APIs in Java, Scala, Python, and R. It extends the MapReduce model to efficiently support more types of computations including **Interactive Queries** and **Stream Processing** with up to **100× faster** performance by exploiting in-memory computation and a directed acyclic graph (DAG) execution engine.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Speed Kitchen

If **Hadoop (MapReduce)** is a kitchen where every ingredient must be fetched from cold storage, prepared, then put back between every dish (slow disk I/O), then **Spark** is a kitchen where all ingredients are kept on the counter (in-memory), and the chef intelligently re-uses chopping boards for multiple recipes (DAG optimization). The result is **100× faster cooking**.

---

## 1.4 NoSQL Databases — Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):**
> NoSQL ("Not Only SQL") databases are non-relational, distributed data storage systems designed for **horizontal scalability**, **flexible schema**, and **high availability**. They sacrifice strict ACID guarantees of relational databases in favor of the **CAP theorem** trade-offs, especially suited for unstructured and semi-structured Big Data workloads.

### Conceptual Analogy: The Filing Cabinet Without a Form

A traditional SQL database is like a rigid Excel spreadsheet where every row must have exactly the same columns (name, age, address). A NoSQL database is like a **flexible folder** where one record can have {name, age} and another {name, address, hobbies, photo} — both can live in the same folder without issue.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hadoop Ecosystem — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Core Components

> [!NOTE]
> **Hadoop has FOUR major pillars** that KTU examiners love to test. Memorize these as a structured 2×2 matrix.

| Layer | Component | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | **HDFS** (Hadoop Distributed File System) | Stores data across cluster nodes |
| **Storage** | **HBase** | NoSQL columnar database on top of HDFS |
| **Processing** | **MapReduce** | Parallel data processing paradigm |
| **Resource Mgmt** | **YARN** (Yet Another Resource Negotiator) | Cluster resource & job scheduling |

---

### 2.1.2 HDFS Architecture (Most Repeated KTU Topic)

**HDFS Block Architecture Logic (Step-by-Step):**

- A file is split into **fixed-size blocks** (default **128 MB** in HDFS, configurable up to **256 MB**).
- Each block is **replicated** across multiple DataNodes (default **replication factor = 3**).
- A **NameNode** (Master) stores metadata: filename, permissions, block → DataNode mapping.
- **DataNodes** (Slaves) store the actual blocks and send heartbeats to the NameNode every **3 seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NameNode is the Single Point of Failure (SPOF)**. The **Secondary NameNode** is NOT a backup — it merely merges **fsimage** and **edit logs** periodically (the *checkpoint* process).

---

### 2.1.3 MapReduce Programming Model

**The two functional pillars:**

1. **Map Function:** $(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_2, v_2)$
2. **Reduce Function:** $(k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(k_3, v_3)$

**Execution Pipeline:**
Input → Splitting → Mapping → Shuffling & Sorting → Reducing → Output

---

### 2.1.4 YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)

| YARN Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **ResourceManager (RM)** | Global arbitrator of cluster resources |
| **NodeManager (NM)** | Per-node agent managing containers |
| **ApplicationMaster (AM)** | Per-application negotiator with RM |
| **Container** | Allocation of CPU + Memory for a task |

---

## 2.2 Apache Spark — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Spark Architecture Layers

```
Spark Driver (Main Process)
        ↓
SparkSession → Cluster Manager → Executors (Worker Nodes)
                          ↓
                   RDD / DataFrame / Dataset
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Spark runs on top of Hadoop** (using YARN as cluster manager) but **Spark does NOT use MapReduce**. Spark has its own DAG-based scheduler called the **DAG Scheduler** that creates optimized execution plans.

### 2.2.2 RDD (Resilient Distributed Dataset)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RDD Properties (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> 1. **R**esilient — Fault-tolerant via lineage (DAG of transformations)
> 2. **D**istributed — Partitioned across cluster nodes
> 3. **D**ataset — Collection of partitioned data
>
> **Five main properties:** Partitions, Dependencies, Compute Function, Partitioner (optional), Preferred Locations (optional)

### 2.2.3 Spark Operations

| Type | Lazy / Eager | Examples | Returns |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Transformations** | Lazy | `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `distinct`, `union`, `join` | New RDD |
| **Actions** | Eager | `count`, `collect`, `reduce`, `take`, `saveAsTextFile` | Value to driver |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students confuse Transformations with Actions. **Transformations are lazy** — they don't execute until an Action is triggered. This is called **lazy evaluation** and is a key Spark optimization.

### 2.2.4 Spark's Speed Advantage

The speed gain comes from:
1. **In-memory computation** — avoids writing intermediate results to HDFS.
2. **DAG optimization** — combines multiple MapReduce stages into a single stage.
3. **Lazy evaluation** — only computes what's required.

---

## 2.3 NoSQL Databases — Deep Dive

### 2.3.1 CAP Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CAP Theorem (Eric Brewer, 2000):**
> In any distributed data store, you can simultaneously guarantee **only TWO** of the following three properties:
> - **C**onsistency — every read sees the latest write
> - **A**vailability — every request gets a response
> - **P**artition tolerance — system continues despite network failures

| CA | CP | AP |
|---|---|---|
| Traditional RDBMS | HBase, MongoDB (limited) | Cassandra, DynamoDB |

### 2.3.2 BASE vs ACID

| Property | ACID (SQL) | BASE (NoSQL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Atomicity** | All or nothing | Partial OK |
| **Consistency** | Strong | Eventual |
| **Isolation** | Strict | Weak |
| **Durability** | Guaranteed | Best effort |

> **ACID** = **B**asic **A**vailability, **S**oft state, **E**ventual consistency (**BASE**)

### 2.3.3 Four Types of NoSQL Databases

| Type | Data Model | Examples | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Key-Value** | Hash table: key → value | Redis, DynamoDB, Riak | Caching, session storage |
| **Document** | JSON/BSON documents | MongoDB, CouchDB | Content management, catalogs |
| **Columnar** | Column families | HBase, Cassandra | Time-series, analytics |
| **Graph** | Nodes + Edges | Neo4j, OrientDB | Social networks, recommendation |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **rapid-revision cheat sheet**. Memorize these for the 3-mark direct questions.

| # | Concept | Key Formula / Rule | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | HDFS Block Size (default) | $B = 128$ MB | MB |
| 2 | Default Replication Factor | $R = 3$ | copies |
| 3 | Storage with Replication | $S_{\text{total}} = D \times R$ | Bytes |
| 4 | HDFS Heartbeat | $T_{hb} = 3$ seconds | sec |
| 5 | MapReduce Throughput | $T = \dfrac{D_{\text{input}}}{N_{\text{mappers}} \cdot t_{\text{map}}}$ | records/sec |
| 6 | Hadoop Speed vs Spark | $S_{\text{spark}} \approx 100 \times S_{\text{MR}}$ | (in-memory) |
| 7 | CAP Combinations | $\vert C \cap A \cap P \vert \leq 2$ | properties |
| 8 | Block Count | $N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \dfrac{D}{B} \right\rceil$ | blocks |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Finance** | Fraud detection using Spark Streaming on transaction logs |
| **Healthcare** | Genomic data analysis using Hadoop HDFS |
| **E-Commerce** | Recommendation engines using Graph NoSQL (Neo4j) |
| **IoT** | Sensor data ingestion via Kafka → Spark → Cassandra |
| **Social Media** | MongoDB for storing posts/comments/feeds |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 HDFS Storage Calculation — Step-by-Step

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently asks: *"A file of size X GB is stored in HDFS. Calculate the number of blocks and total storage used with default replication."*

### Problem Setup
A file of size **$D = 10$ GB** is stored in HDFS with default block size **$B = 128$ MB** and replication factor **$R = 3$**.

### Step 1: Convert units consistently

$$D = 10 \text{ GB} = 10 \times 1024 \text{ MB} = 10240 \text{ MB}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Number of Blocks

$$N_{\text{blocks}} = \left\lceil \frac{D}{B} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{10240}{128} \right\rceil$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{10240}{128} &= 80.0 \\
N_{\text{blocks}} &= 80 \text{ blocks}
\end{aligned}$$

> Each block is replicated **3 times** across different DataNodes.

### Step 3: Calculate Total Logical Storage

The user perceives the file as **10 GB**, but the actual physical storage is:

$$S_{\text{physical}} = D \times R$$

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{\text{physical}} &= 10 \text{ GB} \times 3 \\
&= 30 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Total Number of Block Copies

$$N_{\text{copies}} = N_{\text{blocks}} \times R = 80 \times 3 = 240 \text{ block copies}$$

### Step 5: Validate Block Last Block Size

$$B_{\text{last}} = D - (N_{\text{blocks}} - 1) \times B = 10240 - 79 \times 128$$

$$\begin{aligned}
79 \times 128 &= 10112 \text{ MB} \\
B_{\text{last}} &= 10240 - 10112 = 128 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}$$

Since the file size is an exact multiple of block size, the last block is full (no wasted space).

---

## 3.2 MapReduce Word Count — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks to **trace the MapReduce execution** for a small input.

### Input Documents
- `Doc1: "Big data is big"`
- `Doc2: "Data analytics is fun"`

### Step 1: Splitting Phase
Documents are split into lines (key = line offset, value = line text).

### Step 2: Mapping Phase (each Mapper)
Output of Mapper for Doc1:
$$\text{Map}(k_1, v_1) \rightarrow [(“big”, 1), (“data”, 1), (“is”, 1), (“big”, 1)]$$

Output of Mapper for Doc2:
$$[(“data”, 1), (“analytics”, 1), (“is”, 1), (“fun”, 1)]$$

### Step 3: Shuffling & Sorting Phase (Group by Key)
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{“analytics”} &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{“big”} &\rightarrow [1, 1] \\
\text{“data”} &\rightarrow [1, 1] \\
\text{“fun”} &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{“is”} &\rightarrow [1, 1]
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4: Reducing Phase (Sum values per key)
$$\text{Reduce}(k_2, \text{list}(v_2)) \rightarrow (k_3, v_3)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(\text{“analytics”}, 1) \\
(\text{“big”}, 2) \\
(\text{“data”}, 2) \\
(\text{“fun”}, 1) \\
(\text{“is”}, 2)
\end{aligned}$$

### Final Output
A sorted key-value store where each unique word is mapped to its frequency count.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for KTU)

| Parameter | Hadoop MapReduce | Apache Spark |
|---|---|---|
| **Processing Mode** | Disk-based batch | In-memory |
| **Speed** | Slower | Up to **100× faster** |
| **Ease of Use** | Complex Java code | Python, Scala, R APIs |
| **Real-time Processing** | Limited (via Storm) | Native (Spark Streaming) |
| **Cost** | Cheaper hardware | More RAM needed |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Replication | RDD Lineage |
| **Machine Learning** | Mahout | MLlib |

| Parameter | RDBMS (SQL) | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| **Schema** | Fixed, rigid | Dynamic, flexible |
| **Scalability** | Vertical (bigger server) | Horizontal (more servers) |
| **ACID** | Fully supported | BASE / Eventual consistency |
| **Data Type** | Structured tables | Unstructured / Semi |
| **Joins** | Excellent | Limited or none |
| **Use Case** | Banking, ERP | Social media, IoT |

---

## 3.4 Spark RDD Transformation Chain — Lineage Derivation

Consider the following RDD transformation lineage:

```text
RDD1 = sc.textFile("data.txt")           // Input RDD
RDD2 = RDD1.flatMap(lambda x: x.split(" "))
RDD3 = RDD2.filter(lambda x: x != "")
RDD4 = RDD3.map(lambda x: (x, 1))
RDD5 = RDD4.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)
```

### Lineage DAG (Logical Execution Plan)

$$RDD_1 \xrightarrow{\text{flatMap}} RDD_2 \xrightarrow{\text{filter}} RDD_3 \xrightarrow{\text{map}} RDD_4 \xrightarrow{\text{reduceByKey}} RDD_5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RDD5's Lineage:** if any partition of RDD5 is lost, Spark can recompute it by tracing back through RDD4 → RDD3 → RDD2 → RDD1. This lineage-based recovery is what makes RDDs *resilient*.

### Lazy Evaluation Trigger

Only when an **Action** such as `collect()` or `count()` is called does Spark:
1. Build the DAG.
2. Optimize it via **Catalyst Optimizer**.
3. Submit to **DAG Scheduler** → **Task Scheduler** → **Executors**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HDFS Architecture — Master/Slave Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    ClientA[Client Application]
    NN[NameNode Master]
    SNN[Secondary NameNode]
    DN1[DataNode 1]
    DN2[DataNode 2]
    DN3[DataNode 3]
    DN4[DataNode 4]
    B1A[Block A Copy 1]
    B1B[Block A Copy 2]
    B1C[Block A Copy 3]
    B2A[Block B Copy 1]
    B2B[Block B Copy 2]
    B2C[Block B Copy 3]

    ClientA -->|1 Read Write Request| NN
    NN -->|2 Metadata Response| ClientA
    ClientA -->|3 Direct Data Transfer| DN1
    ClientA -->|3 Direct Data Transfer| DN2
    NN -->|Checkpoint Coordination| SNN
    NN -->|Heartbeat 3s| DN1
    NN -->|Heartbeat 3s| DN2
    DN1 --> B1A
    DN1 --> B2A
    DN2 --> B1B
    DN2 --> B2B
    DN3 --> B1C
    DN3 --> B2C
    DN4 --> B1A
    DN4 --> B2C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Client talks to the NameNode for **metadata** only. The actual block transfer happens **directly between Client and DataNodes**. This is called the **Data Locality / RPC Pipeline** and is a key HDFS optimization.

---

## 4.2 MapReduce Execution Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    InputFile[Input File HDFS]
    Split[Input Split]
    Map1[Mapper 1]
    Map2[Mapper 2]
    Map3[Mapper 3]
    Shuffle[Shuffle and Sort]
    Red1[Reducer 1]
    Red2[Reducer 2]
    Output1[Output HDFS Part 1]
    Output2[Output HDFS Part 2]

    InputFile --> Split
    Split --> Map1
    Split --> Map2
    Split --> Map3
    Map1 --> Shuffle
    Map2 --> Shuffle
    Map3 --> Shuffle
    Shuffle --> Red1
    Shuffle --> Red2
    Red1 --> Output1
    Red2 --> Output2
```

---

## 4.3 Spark Architecture — Driver to Executors

```mermaid
graph TB
    Driver[Spark Driver Program]
    Sess[SparkSession]
    CM[Cluster Manager YARN]
    EX1[Executor JVM Worker 1]
    EX2[Executor JVM Worker 2]
    EX3[Executor JVM Worker 3]
    C1A[Cache Block A]
    C1B[Cache Block B]
    T1[Task Thread 1]
    T2[Task Thread 2]
    T3[Task Thread 3]

    Driver --> Sess
    Sess --> CM
    CM --> EX1
    CM --> EX2
    CM --> EX3
    EX1 --> C1A
    EX1 --> T1
    EX1 --> T2
    EX2 --> C1B
    EX2 --> T3
```

---

## 4.4 CAP Theorem Decision Triangle

```mermaid
graph TB
    C[Consistency]
    A[Availability]
    P[Partition Tolerance]
    CA[CA Systems RDBMS Postgres]
    CP[CP Systems HBase MongoDB]
    AP[AP Systems Cassandra DynamoDB]

    C --- CA
    A --- CA
    C --- CP
    P --- CP
    A --- AP
    P --- AP
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Since **network partitions are inevitable** in distributed systems (P is mandatory), the real choice is between **CP** and **AP** — not all three. KTU examiners test this nuance.

---

## 4.5 NoSQL Database Classification — Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    NoSQL[NoSQL Databases]
    KV[Key Value Store]
    DOC[Document Store]
    COL[Columnar Store]
    GR[Graph Store]

    NoSQL --> KV
    NoSQL --> DOC
    NoSQL --> COL
    NoSQL --> GR

    KV --> KV1[Redis]
    KV --> KV2[DynamoDB]
    DOC --> DOC1[MongoDB]
    DOC --> DOC2[CouchDB]
    COL --> COL1[HBase]
    COL --> COL2[Cassandra]
    GR --> GR1[Neo4j]
    GR --> GR2[OrientDB]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Explain the HDFS architecture with a neat diagram. Differentiate between NameNode and DataNode.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** follows a **Master/Slave architecture**:

- **NameNode (Master):** Stores **metadata** only (file names, permissions, block locations, replication info). It does NOT store actual data. It is the single point of coordination and runs on commodity hardware with high RAM.
- **DataNode (Slaves):** Store **actual data blocks** on local disks. They send **heartbeat signals every 3 seconds** to NameNode and perform block read/write on instruction.

> For high availability, **Secondary NameNode** periodically merges `fsimage` and `edit logs` (checkpointing). It is NOT a hot backup.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Architecture diagram (1 mark)
- NameNode vs DataNode difference (1.5 marks)
- Role of Secondary NameNode (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is the CAP theorem? Explain any two NoSQL databases with suitable examples.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

The **CAP Theorem** (Brewer, 2000) states that a distributed database can guarantee **at most two** of the three properties simultaneously: **C**onsistency, **A**vailability, and **P**artition Tolerance.

**Example 1: MongoDB (Document Store, CP-leaning)**
- Stores data as **BSON documents** (JSON-like).
- Schema-less, supports nested data.
- Use case: content management systems.

**Example 2: Cassandra (Columnar Store, AP-leaning)**
- Wide-column store with **eventual consistency**.
- Masterless architecture → no single point of failure.
- Use case: time-series IoT data, Netflix's recommendation backend.

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **MapReduce programming model** in detail with a suitable example. Trace the execution of a word count program on the input:
- `Doc1: "Hadoop is fast"`
- `Doc2: "Spark is faster"`

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Hadoop ecosystem** with neat diagrams. Explain the role of HDFS, MapReduce, YARN, and HBase.

#### Model Solution (a):

**MapReduce Model (3.5 marks):**
- Developed by Google (Dean & Ghemawat, 2004).
- Two functions: `map()` and `reduce()`, both written by the user.
- The framework handles parallelization, fault tolerance, and data distribution.

**Phases:**
1. **Input Splitting** — file divided into fixed-size splits (default 128 MB).
2. **Mapping** — each split processed by a Mapper producing $(k_2, v_2)$ pairs.
3. **Shuffling & Sorting** — framework groups values by key across mappers.
4. **Reducing** — each Reducer aggregates values for its assigned keys.
5. **Output** — written back to HDFS.

**Worked Trace (3.5 marks):**

**Mapping Phase Output:**
- Mapper1 (Doc1): $\rightarrow$ `(Hadoop,1), (is,1), (fast,1)`
- Mapper2 (Doc2): $\rightarrow$ `(Spark,1), (is,1), (faster,1)`

**Shuffling & Sorting:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{“Hadoop”} &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{“Spark”} &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{“is”} &\rightarrow [1, 1] \\
\text{“fast”} &\rightarrow [1] \\
\text{“faster”} &\rightarrow [1]
\end{aligned}$$

**Reducing Phase Output:**
- `(Hadoop, 1)`, `(Spark, 1)`, `(is, 2)`, `(fast, 1)`, `(faster, 1)`

#### Model Solution (b):

**Hadoop Ecosystem Components (7 marks, 1.5 each):**

| Component | Layer | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **HDFS** | Storage | Distributed, fault-tolerant file system |
| **MapReduce** | Processing | Parallel computation framework |
| **YARN** | Resource Mgmt | Cluster resource scheduling (CPU + RAM) |
| **HBase** | Database | NoSQL columnar store on HDFS |

**YARN Components:** ResourceManager (global), NodeManager (per-node), ApplicationMaster (per-app), Container (resource unit).

**HBase Architecture:** Region Servers, HMaster, Zookeeper coordination, column families stored as HFiles.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write HDFS and MapReduce definitions but **forget the YARN block diagram**. Always include the **ResourceManager → NodeManager → Container** flow for full marks.

---

### QUESTION B — [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **architecture of Apache Spark** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between **RDD transformations and actions** with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What are **NoSQL databases**? Explain the four types of NoSQL databases with examples. Compare NoSQL with RDBMS.

#### Model Solution (a):

**Spark Architecture (4 marks):**
- **Driver Program** runs the main function and creates the `SparkSession`.
- **Cluster Manager** (YARN, Mesos, or Spark Standalone) allocates resources.
- **Executors** are JVM processes on worker nodes that run tasks and store data in memory or disk.
- **Tasks** are units of work sent by the Driver to Executors.

**RDD Properties:** Partitions, Dependencies, Compute Function, Partitioner (optional), Preferred Locations (optional).

**Transformations vs Actions (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Transformations | Actions |
|---|---|---|
| Execution | Lazy | Eager |
| Return | New RDD | Value to Driver / Output |
| Examples | `map`, `filter`, `flatMap`, `join`, `union` | `count`, `collect`, `take`, `reduce` |

**Example:**
- `rdd2 = rdd1.filter(lambda x: x > 10)` → Transformation (lazy)
- `rdd2.count()` → Action (triggers computation)

#### Model Solution (b):

**NoSQL Definition (1 mark):** Non-relational, distributed, schema-flexible databases designed for horizontal scalability.

**Four Types (4 marks, 1 each):**

1. **Key-Value Store** — Redis, DynamoDB. Stores values indexed by unique keys. Ideal for caching and session management.
2. **Document Store** — MongoDB, CouchDB. Stores JSON/BSON documents with nested fields. Ideal for catalogs and CMS.
3. **Columnar Store** — Cassandra, HBase. Stores data in column families instead of rows. Ideal for time-series and analytics.
4. **Graph Store** — Neo4j. Stores nodes and edges. Ideal for social networks and recommendation engines.

**RDBMS vs NoSQL (2 marks):**

| Parameter | RDBMS | NoSQL |
|---|---|---|
| Schema | Fixed | Flexible |
| Scalability | Vertical | Horizontal |
| ACID | Full | Eventual / BASE |
| Query Language | SQL | Varies (MongoQL, CQL) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** In CAP theorem answers, students often **write only the full forms** without explaining the **trade-off implication**. Always end with the sentence: *"Since P is mandatory in distributed systems, the real choice is between CP and AP."* — this earns the concluding 1 mark.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4**

- ☐ **HDFS:** Master/Slave, block size = 128 MB, replication = 3, heartbeat = 3 sec, NameNode = metadata only.
- ☐ **Secondary NameNode** = NOT a backup, only does checkpointing of `fsimage` + `edit logs`.
- ☐ **MapReduce** = `map()` → `shuffle & sort` → `reduce()`. Java-based, disk-heavy, batch only.
- ☐ **YARN** = ResourceManager, NodeManager, ApplicationMaster, Container.
- ☐ **Spark** = in-memory, **100× faster** than MapReduce, supports DAG, streaming, ML, graph.
- ☐ **RDD** = Resilient (lineage), Distributed, Dataset. **Transformations are lazy; Actions are eager.**
- ☐ **Lazy Evaluation** = Spark builds DAG only when an action is called.
- ☐ **CAP Theorem** = Only 2 of {C, A, P} achievable; P is mandatory → choose between CP and AP.
- ☐ **ACID** = RDBMS; **BASE** = NoSQL (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent).
- ☐ **Four NoSQL Types** = Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Columnar (Cassandra/HBase), Graph (Neo4j).
- ☐ **Hadoop** = storage + batch processing; **Spark** = in-memory processing; **NoSQL** = flexible distributed storage.
- ☐ **Spark MLlib** = machine learning library; **Spark Streaming** = micro-batch stream processing.
- ☐ **HBase** = NoSQL columnar DB on top of HDFS; **Cassandra** = masterless AP system.
- ☐ **Horizontal scaling** = add more machines; **Vertical scaling** = add more RAM/CPU to one machine.

**High-Yield Mnemonic for Hadoop vs Spark:**
> *"**H**adoop **H**ides **D**ata **S**lowly"* vs *"**S**park **S**peeds **U**p **R**eal-time **K**nowledge"*

**Memory trick for CAP:**
> *"You can have **C**ake (Consistency) or **A**pple (Availability), but if the **P**arty (Partition) splits, you must choose."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Data Processing: Streaming Data, Apache Kafka
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT635 - DATA ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Data Processing: Streaming Data, Apache Kafka

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Real-Time Data Processing** is a computational paradigm in which data is ingested, processed, and analyzed within strict time constraints (typically **milliseconds to seconds**) after the data is produced. It is the operational backbone of modern data analytics, enabling immediate reaction to events as they occur rather than waiting for scheduled batch windows.

**Streaming Data** is a continuous, unbounded, time-ordered sequence of data records (events) that are generated at high velocity from heterogeneous sources such as IoT sensors, web clickstreams, financial tickers, social media feeds, server logs, and mobile applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Standard):** Streaming data is a perpetual flow of small, independent, time-stamped events that must be processed incrementally — one record or a small micro-batch at a time — without the luxury of having the entire dataset available before computation begins.

**Apache Kafka** is a distributed, horizontally-scalable, fault-tolerant **publish–subscribe messaging system** originally developed by LinkedIn (2010–2011) and now an open-source **Apache Software Foundation** project. It is engineered to act as a high-throughput, low-latency **distributed commit log** for real-time data pipelines, streaming applications, and event-driven microservices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office + Newspaper" Model

To intuitively understand streaming data and Kafka, imagine a **modern national newspaper distribution network**:

| Real-World Analogy | Kafka Equivalent | Role in System |
|---|---|---|
| Newspaper printing press | **Kafka Producer** | Generates and publishes content |
| Named editions (Sports, Business, City) | **Topics** | Logical channels of information |
| Delivery trucks / distribution routes | **Partitions** | Parallel physical pathways |
| Regional warehouses | **Brokers (servers)** | Store and forward messages |
| Home subscribers | **Kafka Consumers** | Read specific editions |
| Subscription record | **Consumer Group / Offset** | Tracks reading position |

> Just as newspapers never stop being printed and subscribers can pick up the latest edition at any time, Kafka never stops accepting messages, and consumers can read at their own pace, replaying from any point in history.

- **Batch Processing = A monthly magazine**: Data is collected over a month, then printed and distributed all at once.
- **Real-Time Streaming = A live news ticker**: Information is published the instant an event happens and consumed immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Batch processing operates on **bounded** (finite) datasets with high latency, while streaming processing operates on **unbounded** (infinite) datasets with **sub-second latency**.

## 1.3 Standard Streaming Metrics (Board-Expected Terminology)

The following metrics are **mandatory** vocabulary for KTU answers:

- **Throughput** — Number of events processed per unit time (e.g., **1 million messages/second**).
- **Latency** — End-to-end time delay between event creation and processing completion (e.g., **< 10 ms**).
- **Durability** — Guarantee that published data will not be lost (Kafka achieves this via replication).
- **Event-Time vs. Processing-Time** — The moment an event *occurred* in the real world vs. the moment the system *observes* it.
- **At-Most-Once / At-Least-Once / Exactly-Once** — Three delivery semantics with progressively stronger guarantees.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Real-Time Data Processing

A streaming system must simultaneously satisfy three architectural requirements. KTU questions frequently test whether students can articulate these clearly.

### Pillar 1 — Continuous Ingestion
- Data sources (producers) push events **asynchronously** and **continuously** into the system.
- The system must handle **backpressure** — when consumers cannot keep up with the producer's rate.
- **Backpressure strategies**: drop, buffer (spill to disk), or apply load shedding.

### Pillar 2 — Stateful Stream Processing
- Unlike stateless map/filter functions, real systems maintain **state** (counters, windows, aggregations).
- State is checkpointed to fault-tolerant storage (e.g., **RocksDB + Kafka's internal `__consumer_offsets` topic**).

### Pillar 3 — Event-Time Semantics
- Events carry their own timestamps from the source device.
- **Watermarks** are inserted to indicate "no events older than time *T* will arrive" — this is the mechanism that handles out-of-order arrival.

## 2.2 Apache Kafka — Deep Architectural Analysis

Kafka follows a **distributed commit-log** architecture. The core data structure is an **append-only log** per partition.

### Step-by-Step Working Logic

1. **Producer publishes** a record to a logical **Topic**.
2. The topic is split into one or more **Partitions** (Kafka's unit of parallelism).
3. Kafka uses a **partitioner** to decide which partition receives a record (default: hash of key; round-robin if no key).
4. The record is appended to the **end** of the partition's log file on disk.
5. The record receives a monotonically increasing **offset** (a 64-bit integer) per partition.
6. Records are **replicated** to multiple brokers based on the **replication factor** (typically 3).
7. **Consumers** read records in offset order, tracking their own position (offset) so restarts are seamless.
8. Retention policy (time or size) governs how long records are kept — defaults to **7 days**.

## 2.3 Kafka Core Components — High-Yield Reference

| Component | Role | KTU Exam Hint |
|---|---|---|
| **Producer** | Publishes records to topics | Always paired with serializer config |
| **Consumer** | Subscribes and processes records | Belongs to a *Consumer Group* |
| **Broker** | A single Kafka server (JVM process) | Cluster = N brokers |
| **Topic** | Logical feed / category of records | Must have ≥ 1 partition |
| **Partition** | Ordered, immutable sequence of records | Unit of parallelism |
| **Offset** | Unique per-partition record ID | Monotonically increasing |
| **Consumer Group** | Set of consumers cooperating on a topic | One partition → one consumer in group |
| **ZooKeeper / KRaft** | Cluster coordination service | Modern Kafka uses KRaft (no ZooKeeper) |
| **Leader / Follower** | Replica roles per partition | Only leader serves reads/writes |
| **ISR (In-Sync Replicas)** | Replicas caught up with leader | Determines write durability |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations and metrics are routinely tested in KTU ESE module-level questions.

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Typical Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N_{\text{msg}}}{T_{\text{window}}}$ | Messages processed per second | msgs/sec |
| 2 | $L_{\text{E2E}} = L_{\text{prod}} + L_{\text{broker}} + L_{\text{cons}}$ | End-to-end latency decomposition | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| 3 | $S_{\text{storage}} = R \times \bar{m} \times T_{\text{retention}}$ | Total storage required; $R$ = msg rate, $\bar{m}$ = avg size, $T_{\text{retention}}$ = retention time | **Bytes / GB / TB** |
| 4 | $P_{\text{max-parallelism}} = \min(N_{\text{partitions}}, N_{\text{consumers}})$ | Max parallelism in a consumer group | Integer |
| 5 | $N_{\text{replicas}} = R_f \times N_{\text{brokers-hold-copy}}$ | Replication footprint | Integer |
| 6 | $C_{\text{total-cluster}} = N_{\text{brokers}} \times C_{\text{per-broker}}$ | Aggregate cluster CPU/memory | Cores / GB RAM |
| 7 | $\text{Watermark}(t) = t - \epsilon$ | Event-time progress marker with allowed lateness $\epsilon$ | Time |
| 8 | $T_{\text{rebalance}} \propto \dfrac{1}{N_{\text{partitions}}}$ | Time to rebalance consumer group | Seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When asked "What is the maximum parallelism of a Kafka consumer group?" — the correct answer is $\min(\text{partitions}, \text{consumers})$, **not** the number of consumers. Excess consumers remain **idle**.

## 2.5 Delivery Semantics — KTU Board-Favorite Topic

| Semantic | Guarantee | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **At-most-once** | Records may be lost, never duplicated | Metrics where loss is acceptable |
| **At-least-once** | Records never lost, may be duplicated | Default in Kafka |
| **Exactly-once** | Records processed once and only once | Financial transactions, billing |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **LinkedIn** — original use case: activity tracking and operational metrics.
- **Netflix** — real-time event pipeline for monitoring and recommendations.
- **Uber** — matching riders to drivers via streaming trip events.
- **Banks** — fraud detection within milliseconds of transaction.
- **IoT** — sensor telemetry ingestion from millions of devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency Decomposition

A KTU favourite: derive the total latency a record experiences as it travels from a producer to a consumer.

**Step 1 — Identify the three sequential delay components.**

When a producer publishes a record, the record traverses three stages before being processed by a consumer:

1. Producer-side delay: serialization, network send, and acknowledgement wait.
2. Broker-side delay: append to partition log, replication to followers, and fsync to disk.
3. Consumer-side delay: fetch from broker, deserialization, and user-defined processing.

**Step 2 — Express each component formally.**

Let us denote:

- $L_{\text{prod}}$ = time taken by the producer to publish and receive acknowledgement.
- $L_{\text{broker}}$ = time for the leader broker to append, replicate, and persist the record.
- $L_{\text{cons}}$ = time for the consumer to fetch, deserialize, and complete processing.

**Step 3 — Sum the components to get total end-to-end latency.**

$$
L_{\text{E2E}} = L_{\text{prod}} + L_{\text{broker}} + L_{\text{cons}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute typical real-world values.**

For a healthy Kafka cluster: $L_{\text{prod}} \approx 2$ ms, $L_{\text{broker}} \approx 5$ ms, $L_{\text{cons}} \approx 3$ ms.

$$
L_{\text{E2E}} = 2 + 5 + 3 = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**

A total latency of **10 ms** is well within real-time requirements. KTU will accept answers quoting "$L_{\text{E2E}} < 100$ ms" as a real-time threshold.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question says "explain real-time processing in Kafka," the latency formula $L_{\text{E2E}} = L_{\text{prod}} + L_{\text{broker}} + L_{\text{cons}}$ is often the difference between a 7-mark and a 14-mark answer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Capacity Planning

A KTU 2024 Scheme question may give a producer rate and ask for the disk storage needed.

**Problem statement:** A Kafka cluster receives $R = 50{,}000$ messages/second. The average message size is $\bar{m} = 1$ KB. The retention policy is $T_{\text{retention}} = 7$ days. The replication factor is $R_f = 3$. Compute total storage required across the cluster.

**Step 1 — Convert retention time into seconds.**

$$
T_{\text{retention}} = 7 \text{ days} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 604{,}800 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total messages stored in one replica.**

$$
N_{\text{msg}} = R \times T_{\text{retention}} = 50{,}000 \times 604{,}800 = 3.024 \times 10^{10} \text{ messages}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute raw data size per replica in bytes.**

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = N_{\text{msg}} \times \bar{m} = 3.024 \times 10^{10} \times 1024 \text{ bytes} = 3.096576 \times 10^{13} \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to terabytes.**

$$
S_{\text{raw}} = \dfrac{3.096576 \times 10^{13}}{1024^{4}} \approx 28.16 \text{ TB (per replica)}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the replication factor for cluster-wide storage.**

$$
S_{\text{total}} = S_{\text{raw}} \times R_f = 28.16 \times 3 \approx 84.48 \text{ TB}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

The cluster needs approximately **85 TB** of usable storage (plus overhead for OS, logs, indexes — usually add 20–30%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to multiply by the replication factor. The board explicitly deducts marks for this.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Consumer Parallelism

**Problem statement:** A topic has $N_p = 8$ partitions. Three consumer groups (A, B, C) are configured with $N_{c,A} = 4$, $N_{c,B} = 8$, $N_{c,C} = 12$ consumers respectively. Determine the active consumers and idle consumers in each group.

**Step 1 — Apply the parallelism rule.**

$$
P_{\text{max}} = \min(N_{\text{partitions}}, N_{\text{consumers}})
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate for Group A.**

$$
P_{A} = \min(8, 4) = 4 \text{ active}, \quad I_{A} = 0 \text{ idle}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for Group B.**

$$
P_{B} = \min(8, 8) = 8 \text{ active}, \quad I_{B} = 0 \text{ idle}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for Group C.**

$$
P_{C} = \min(8, 12) = 8 \text{ active}, \quad I_{C} = 12 - 8 = 4 \text{ idle}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering insight.**

Adding consumers beyond the partition count gives **zero benefit**. To scale Group C, you must **increase the topic's partition count first** — a critical KTU insight.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Batch vs. Real-Time

| Parameter | Batch Processing | Real-Time Streaming (Kafka) |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | **Bounded** (finite) | **Unbounded** (infinite) |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | **Milliseconds to seconds** |
| Throughput | Very high | High |
| Storage | Process and discard | Retain for replay |
| Tools | Hadoop, Spark batch | Kafka, Flink, Spark Streaming |
| Recovery | Re-run the job | Replay from any offset |
| Use case | Monthly billing report | Fraud detection |
| Cost model | Pay for compute per job | Always-on cluster |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Apache Kafka High-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **distributed commit-log architecture** of an Apache Kafka cluster. It shows producers, the broker cluster, the topic-partition structure, replication, and consumer groups.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Web App Producer] -->|publish| T1
    P2[IoT Sensor Producer] -->|publish| T1
    P3[Mobile App Producer] -->|publish| T1
    P4[Log Service Producer] -->|publish| T1

    T1{{Topic: UserEvents}}
    T1 --> PA[Partition 0]
    T1 --> PB[Partition 1]
    T1 --> PC[Partition 2]

    PA --> B1[Broker 1 - Leader]
    PA --> B2[Broker 2 - Follower]
    PA --> B3[Broker 3 - Follower]

    PB --> B2[Broker 2 - Leader]
    PB --> B3[Broker 3 - Follower]
    PB --> B1[Broker 1 - Follower]

    PC --> B3[Broker 3 - Leader]
    PC --> B1[Broker 1 - Follower]
    PC --> B2[Broker 2 - Follower]

    B1 --> G1
    B2 --> G1
    B3 --> G1

    G1{{Consumer Group: Analytics}}
    G1 --> CA[Consumer A]
    G1 --> CB[Consumer B]
    G1 --> CC[Consumer C]

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2 fill:#cce5ff
    style P3 fill:#cce5ff
    style P4 fill:#cce5ff
    style T1 fill:#ffe599
    style PA fill:#d9ead3
    style PB fill:#d9ead3
    style PC fill:#d9ead3
    style B1 fill:#f4cccc
    style B2 fill:#f4cccc
    style B3 fill:#f4cccc
    style G1 fill:#fff2cc
    style CA fill:#d9d2e9
    style CB fill:#d9d2e9
    style CC fill:#d9d2e9
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Four independent **producers** (web, IoT, mobile, logs) write events into a single logical **Topic** named `UserEvents`.
- The topic is divided into **three partitions (P0, P1, P2)** — these are the units of parallelism.
- Each partition is **replicated** across three brokers: one **leader** (accepts writes) and two **followers** (passive copies).
- A **Consumer Group** named `Analytics` contains three consumers — Kafka assigns exactly one partition per consumer.

---

## 4.2 Internal Anatomy of a Kafka Partition (Append-Only Log)

The second diagram illustrates the **append-only log** structure of a single partition. This is the foundational data structure that gives Kafka its durability, ordering, and replay capabilities.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Partition0 [Partition 0 - Append Only Log]
        O0[Offset 0] --- O1[Offset 1] --- O2[Offset 2] --- O3[Offset 3] --- O4[Offset 4] --- O5[Offset 5] --- O6[Offset 6] --- ON[Offset N - HEAD]
    end

    CPos[Consumer Position: Offset 4] -.-> O4
    ProducerApp[New Producer Write] -.-> ON

    style O0 fill:#fce5cd
    style O1 fill:#fce5cd
    style O2 fill:#fce5cd
    style O3 fill:#fce5cd
    style O4 fill:#b6d7a8
    style O5 fill:#fce5cd
    style O6 fill:#fce5cd
    style ON fill:#ea9999
    style CPos fill:#fff2cc
    style ProducerApp fill:#c9daf8
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Records are **appended** sequentially to the right end of the log; older records are on the left.
- Each record has a unique **offset** (0, 1, 2, … N) within its partition.
- The **consumer's position** (offset 4) is independent of the producer's HEAD — the consumer can rewind or fast-forward.
- A **new producer write** always lands at the current HEAD (Offset N), guaranteeing ordering within a partition.

---

## 4.3 Real-Time Streaming Data Pipeline (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps an **end-to-end real-time analytics pipeline** powered by Kafka. Use this when KTU asks you to "draw" a streaming architecture.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Web Clickstream] -->|HTTP events| IN
    S2[IoT Sensors] -->|MQTT events| IN
    S3[Mobile Apps] -->|SDK events| IN
    S4[DB CDC] -->|Change Data Capture| IN

    IN{{Kafka Cluster - 3 Brokers - 12 Partitions}}

    IN --> D1[Stream Processor - Kafka Streams]
    IN --> D2[Stream Processor - Apache Flink]
    IN --> D3[Real-Time Database - Cassandra]

    D1 --> OL[Operational Dashboard - Grafana]
    D2 --> ML[ML Inference Service]
    D2 --> DL[Data Lake - S3 - Long-term Storage]

    style S1 fill:#cfe2f3
    style S2 fill:#cfe2f3
    style S3 fill:#cfe2f3
    style S4 fill:#cfe2f3
    style IN fill:#ffd966
    style D1 fill:#b6d7a8
    style D2 fill:#b6d7a8
    style D3 fill:#b6d7a8
    style OL fill:#d9d2e9
    style ML fill:#d9d2e9
    style DL fill:#d9d2e9
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Sources (left)**: four categories of streaming sources feed events into Kafka.
- **Kafka Cluster (center, yellow)**: the central nervous system, acting as a durable buffer and replayable log.
- **Stream Processors (green)**: Kafka Streams and Apache Flink consume, transform, and enrich events.
- **Sinks (right)**: processed data lands in dashboards, ML services, and long-term storage for downstream analytics.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define streaming data. How is it different from traditional batch data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Streaming data** is a continuous, unbounded, time-ordered sequence of data records generated at high velocity from sources such as IoT sensors, web logs, and financial transactions. It must be processed incrementally in near real-time.

**Differences from batch data:**

| Aspect | Batch Data | Streaming Data |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Bounded / finite | Unbounded / infinite |
| Latency | Minutes to hours | **Milliseconds to seconds** |
| Processing | Periodic jobs | Continuous micro-batches or record-by-record |
| Storage assumption | Entire dataset fits in storage | Data must be processed without full retention |
| Example | Daily sales report | Real-time fraud detection |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the comparison table — exact KTU mark split.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List and briefly explain any three core components of Apache Kafka.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Producer (1 mark)** — A client application that publishes (writes) records to one or more Kafka topics. Producers choose which partition to send records to using a partitioner (default: hash of key).

2. **Consumer (1 mark)** — A client application that subscribes to one or more topics and processes the records. Consumers belong to a **Consumer Group** so that records are load-balanced across multiple instances.

3. **Broker (1 mark)** — A single Kafka server (JVM process) that stores partition logs, services client requests, and replicates data to other brokers. A Kafka **cluster** is a collection of brokers working together.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Module 4, KTU 2024 Scheme]`
**(a) Explain the architecture of Apache Kafka in detail. Describe the role of producers, consumers, brokers, topics, partitions, and consumer groups with a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Apache Kafka follows a **distributed publish–subscribe architecture** built around an append-only commit log. The architecture can be explained through its layered components:

**1. Producers (1 mark)**
